pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient has ESRD (N18.6) with dependence on hemodialysis (Z99.2) and CKD stage 5 (N18.5) complicated by CKD-related anemia (D63.1) and persistent fatigue (R53.83). Following transplant committee review and completion of required pre-transplant testing, renal transplantation with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) is requested; perioperative planning accounts for comorbid atherosclerotic heart disease (I25.10) and hypertension (I10).

pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved renal function with ability to discontinue chronic dialysis and improved functional status while maintaining careful perioperative cardiovascular management.

225.bio_narrative: Meredith Grey is a 45-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who has remained actively engaged in her health care while balancing a demanding professional life and family responsibilities. Over the past several years she has managed chronic cardiovascular conditions including atherosclerotic heart disease (I25.10), essential hypertension (I10), and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). She has been consistent with prescribed risk-reduction therapy including metoprolol, losartan, and atorvastatin, and previously completed a course of cardiac rehabilitation to support endurance and conditioning. In late 2025 and early 2026 she began to notice worsening fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and generalized weakness that were no longer explained by work stress alone. At the 01-20-2026 specialist visit she reported persistent low energy, difficulty completing routine daily activities, and prolonged recovery after exertion. Subsequent evaluation demonstrated progression to chronic kidney disease stage 5 (N18.5) and end stage renal disease (N18.6) requiring ongoing in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2). pa_request.clinical_justification:
Persistent chronic diarrhea with abdominal pain/cramping and change in bowel habits despite conservative management, plus high-risk family history of colorectal cancer. Diagnostic colonoscopy is requested to directly evaluate the colon for inflammatory or neoplastic pathology and guide treatment planning while coordinating periprocedural management of cardiovascular prevention medications.

Objective evidence: 02-05-2026: Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, BUN 12 mg/dL, CO2 25 mmol/L, Potassium 4.0 mmol/L | 02-05-2026: Hgb 12.9 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: Z80.0 - Family history of malignant neoplasm of digestive organs
pa_request.previous_treatments: Dietary trial and probiotic regimen; past short-course loperamide; ongoing psyllium and PRN dicyclomine; hydration support with oral rehydration solution.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Diagnostic clarification of colonic etiology through direct visualization and sampling as indicated to guide next-step management and risk assessment.

263.bio_narrative: Rachel Green is a 45-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who has been proactive about preventive health and typically maintains an active routine despite a demanding career. In late 2025 she developed frequent loose stools and crampy abdominal pain that persisted beyond several weeks and gradually became a chronic pattern. She describes a distinct change in bowel habits (R19.4) compared with her prior baseline, with recurring diarrhea (R19.7) and intermittent abdominal cramping/pain (R10.9). She has remained attentive to hydration and symptom tracking and has communicated frequently with her care team when symptoms persisted. Initial management began with a structured dietary trial and a probiotic regimen started at the 12-15-2025 office visit. During early symptom flares she used a short course of loperamide and continued the probiotic, but diarrhea recurred after the brief improvement. Ongoing symptom-directed therapies were later adjusted to include daily psyllium fiber to bulk stool and dicyclomine as needed for cramping. Basic bloodwork (CBC and CMP on 02-05-2026) was obtained to evaluate for anemia, infection, and metabolic contributors; results did not reveal a clear etiology. Abdominal ultrasound on 02-10-2026 did not show an acute hepatobiliary or renal explanation for her pain. In May 2026, inflammatory and stool screening (CRP and fecal calprotectin) was collected as symptoms continued despite conservative measures. A key feature of her presentation is a high-risk family history of colorectal cancer (Z80.0), which raises concern when paired with persistent symptoms and a sustained change from baseline bowel habits. She was evaluated by gastroenterology on 04-28-2026, where the overall clinical picture supported proceeding with diagnostic colonoscopy (CPT 45378) to directly assess the colon and obtain targeted sampling if indicated. Cardiovascular risk prevention is managed with daily aspirin and atorvastatin, and medication planning for the procedure has been discussed to ensure safe preparation. Socially, Rachel does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and exercises regularly when symptoms allow. She has a reliable support system for transportation and recovery on the day of the scheduled procedure. The colonoscopy is planned for 06-21-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital, Endoscopy Center, to clarify diagnosis and guide next steps in management based on objective findings.

Plan: Schedule colonoscopy for 06-21-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital, Endoscopy Center. Start dicyclomine PRN for cramping. Continue psyllium. Order pre-procedure inflammatory and stool screening to complement workup while awaiting colonoscopy.

261.bio_narrative: Chandler Bing is a 56-year-old man living in Houston, Texas, followed primarily by Dr. Ross Geller, MD at Houston Methodist Hospital. He has a cardiovascular history notable for coronary artery disease with a cardiac stent placed on 06-15-2024, along with chronic hypertension and hypercholesterolemia managed with metoprolol and atorvastatin. He remains engaged in his care, completed cardiac rehabilitation after his stent, and reports dependable medication adherence. He is not a smoker and drinks alcohol moderately in social settings; he exercises routinely and tries to follow mindful eating habits, though recent symptoms have made meals less comfortable. Over the past several months (since late 2025), he developed frequent heartburn and indigestion with epigastric discomfort. At a primary care visit on 01-05-2026, symptoms were documented as regular and bothersome, prompting continuation of proton pump inhibitor therapy and lifestyle counseling. Despite taking omeprazole 20 mg daily before breakfast and using famotidine at night for breakthrough symptoms, he continued to experience daily reflux and upper abdominal pain. By follow-up on 03-14-2026, he reported minimal improvement despite adherence, and he added antacid therapy for symptomatic episodes. A non-invasive diagnostic evaluation was pursued to assess other causes of his pain: an abdominal ultrasound on 02-20-2026 did not show gallstones or biliary dilation, and a laboratory blood panel on 02-22-2026 did not show significant anemia or metabolic derangements. A stool analysis on 02-25-2026 did not reveal an infectious source. In May 2026, his clinical picture evolved with alarm features: he reported intermittent dysphagia (a sensation of food sticking) and unintentional weight loss. At a gastroenterology specialist consultation on 05-12-2026, these symptoms were documented alongside ongoing K21.9 GERD symptoms, R10.13 epigastric pain, and R12 heartburn. Given persistent symptoms despite medical therapy and the presence of R13.10 dysphagia and R63.4 weight loss, an Upper GI Endoscopy with biopsy is planned for 06-21-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital. The goal is to directly evaluate the esophageal, gastric, and duodenal mucosa for conditions that require visualization and tissue sampling. His cardiac history and current aspirin use were reviewed in pre-procedure planning to coordinate medication timing while pursuing timely evaluation of these upper GI alarm symptoms.

260.bio_narrative: John Dorian is a 55-year-old man living in Houston, Texas who established care for frequent heartburn and upper abdominal discomfort that began to interfere with meals and sleep. He works full-time in a hospital setting and reports a structured routine with strong social support from close friends and coworkers. Over the past several months he has experienced near-daily burning reflux symptoms and episodic epigastric pain, often worse after coffee, late dinners, or larger meals. He also describes intermittent nausea with occasional non-bloody vomiting during flares. He denies melena or hematemesis and has not had prior upper endoscopy in this record. He initially presented on 01-15-2026 reporting daily heartburn and upset stomach and began symptom-directed management. At follow-up on 02-10-2026, he continued to report persistent reflux and an “irritated” stomach despite ongoing therapy, leading to additional medication support for nausea and pain while avoiding NSAIDs due to reflux symptoms. A pre-op evaluation on 03-15-2026 included a baseline chest X-ray for sedation planning. On 05-07-2026, he was seen in gastroenterology consultation for persistent symptoms characterized by K21.9 (GERD), R10.13 (epigastric pain), K30 (functional dyspepsia as a working diagnosis), and R11.2 (nausea with vomiting, unspecified). Pre-procedure labs (CBC/CMP/PT-INR) were obtained on 05-10-2026 to support procedural readiness. Socially, he reports no tobacco or illicit drug use and drinks alcohol mainly on weekends. He describes a highly active exercise pattern with moderate-to-vigorous aerobic workouts about 5 days per week and occasional strength training. Family history includes a father with chronic reflux symptoms and a maternal aunt with peptic ulcer disease, which contributes to his interest in clarifying whether there is underlying mucosal disease. He completed a course of CBT to improve coping and reduce stress-related symptom amplification, and he also met briefly with a dietitian to address dietary triggers. Given persistent symptoms affecting daily function and sleep, he is scheduled for Upper GI Endoscopy with biopsy (CPT 43239) on 06-21-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital to directly evaluate for esophagitis, gastritis, or ulcer disease and to obtain biopsies to guide subsequent management.

262.bio_narrative: Maxine Black is a 35-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who has generally been healthy and physically active, maintaining a balanced diet and regular exercise. She works in an office-based role and stays connected with friends and family for support. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol sparingly. Over the past year, she has focused on lifestyle approaches to keep her symptoms manageable, including hydration and meal planning, and she typically uses over-the-counter ibuprofen only for occasional headaches. Beginning in early-to-mid 2025, Maxine developed intermittent lower abdominal cramping that comes in waves and tends to worsen during constipation flares. She describes a change from her prior bowel pattern with variable stool form and frequency (R19.4), and she frequently experiences straining consistent with constipation (K59.00). The cramping is localized to the lower abdomen (R10.30) and has interfered with comfort at work and social activities during flare-ups. Initial evaluation in primary care led to conservative measures including fiber supplementation (psyllium), dietary changes, and a brief adjunct probiotic trial. She also trialed dicyclomine as needed for cramping. While she noted partial improvement with more consistent diet habits, symptoms continued to recur. In 2026, her primary care follow-up addressed ongoing constipation episodes and cramping despite these measures, and an osmotic laxative regimen was discussed alongside stool softener use. As part of the diagnostic pathway prior to the planned colonoscopy, she completed a CBC/CMP (05-06-2026), stool FIT testing (05-07-2026), and an abdominal and pelvic ultrasound (05-09-2026), which did not identify an acute alternative explanation for her symptoms. There is no documented prior colonoscopy in her record, and no family history of colorectal cancer has been recorded in available notes. Given persistence of symptoms over multiple visits with conservative management and pre-procedure diagnostic workup completed, she is scheduled for a diagnostic colonoscopy (CPT 45378) on 06-21-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital to directly evaluate for colonic mucosal or structural contributors and to guide subsequent management planning.

213.bio_narrative: Chandler Bing is a 37-year-old male living in Dallas, Texas with a history of essential hypertension (I10) and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). Over the past several months he has developed intermittent substernal chest tightness provoked by exertion (R07.89), especially during jogging, brisk walking, or climbing multiple flights of stairs. The episodes typically last a few minutes, are relieved by rest, and are accompanied by mild shortness of breath when they occur. He has been consistent with prescribed metoprolol and atorvastatin and has continued weekly cardiac rehabilitation, but he reports that he sometimes limits exercise intensity to avoid triggering symptoms. His initial evaluation on 12/10/2025 documented exertional chest discomfort occurring during workouts and prompted a structured diagnostic workup. He completed an exercise treadmill stress test on 02/02/2026 (CPT 93015), during which his typical chest tightness was reproduced and ECG changes raised concern for ischemia; this is captured as an abnormal cardiovascular function study (R94.39). A follow-up resting ECG on 02/08/2026 (CPT 93000) demonstrated nonspecific ST-T abnormalities. In a cardiology specialist consultation on ... (truncated)

223.procedure_requested: Insertion of tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with ESRD on hemodialysis has repeated AV fistula dysfunction with low flow and recurrent machine alarms resulting in shortened/inadequate dialysis sessions. A tunneled dialysis catheter is requested to establish reliable long-term access in the setting of ongoing access complications and comorbid hypertensive CKD and CKD-related anemia.

Objective evidence: 04-03-2026: Creatinine 9.8, BUN 62, CO2 20, Potassium 5.2, Hgb 9.6 | 02-05-2026: Creatinine 9.4 mg/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: T82.898A - Other specified complications of vascular prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, initial encounter
I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Completed AV fistula creation (05-15-2025) and ongoing dialysis-unit access troubleshooting with repeated cannulation and flow/pressure adjustments; persistent inability to maintain ordered blood flow and complete prescribed treatment time. Past medication adjustments include prior diuretic use (furosemide) and prior ESA regimen (Mircera) switched to epoetin alfa.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Reliable long-term hemodialysis access to allow completion of prescribed treatments with stable blood flow and fewer access-related interruptions.

223.bio_narrative: Caroline Channing is a 40-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas with end stage renal disease (N18.6) requiring thrice-weekly in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2). She has long-standing hypertension complicated by hypertensive chronic kidney disease with ESRD (I12.0) and receives anemia management for chronic kidney disease (D63.1). She has remained engaged in her care, consistently taking her renal medications, tracking symptoms after dialysis, and following a renal-friendly, low-sodium diet. Her current clinical course centers on progressive failure of her left upper extremity AV fistula that was created on 05-15-2025. Beginning in late 2025 and worsening through early 2026, she developed repeated access-related machine alarms during dialysis, difficulty sustaining ordered blood flow rates, and sessions that ended early due to unstable pressures. She reports fatigue and reduced stamina after these shortened runs, along with intermittent post-dialysis nausea that responds to ondansetron. The dialysis unit documented multiple episodes of low arterial and high venous pressure alarms with repeated needle repositioning, reduced pump speeds, and incomplete prescribed treatment times, consistent with a complication of vascular access (T82.898A). She has undergone a structured evaluation and escalation of care across multiple encounters. A specialist consult on 02-01-2026 initiated a renewed access workup after she reported repeated access failures. A chest X-ray (02-10-2026) and vascular access duplex ultrasound (04-01-2026) were obtained to support planning for alternate access, with duplex findings correlating with low-flow dysfunction. Laboratory review including BMP/CBC/coagulation (04-03-2026) supports peri-procedural planning and reflects anemia consistent with CKD. Despite continued dialysis-unit troubleshooting and monitoring, her access problems persisted across follow-ups through 04-15-2026. Caroline works in hospitality management with a variable schedule; she relies on a close friend for transportation on dialysis days and maintains stable housing. She has no tobacco history, drinks alcohol sparingly at social events, and denies illicit drug use. Family history is notable for hypertension in both parents and a maternal uncle with kidney disease requiring dialysis in his 60s. Given repeated inability to complete prescribed dialysis and documented access dysfunction, she was referred for insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) to provide reliable long-term access while ongoing fistula management and future permanent access planning proceed.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of recurrent AV fistula access problems causing shortened dialysis sessions and inadequate blood flow.
encounters.chief_complaint: My fistula sites keep failing and dialysis keeps getting cut short.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 40-year-old female with ESRD on hemodialysis presents for vascular access evaluation. She reports increasing difficulty with cannulation over the past several months and frequent dialysis machine alarms leading to shortened treatments. She notes increased fatigue after incomplete runs and intermittent nausea after dialysis, improved with ondansetron. No fever, chills, or drainage from the access site.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and post-dialysis nausea. Denies chest pain, dyspnea at rest, cough, abdominal pain, melena, or focal neurologic deficits. No erythema or purulence at access site.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing, no acute distress. Cardiovascular: regular rhythm. Lungs clear. Left upper extremity AV fistula present with diminished thrill compared to baseline per patient; no overlying warmth, erythema, or drainage. No significant edema.

Assessment: ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) with complication of vascular access (T82.898A) manifested by recurrent alarms and inability to maintain prescribed dialysis blood flow. Hypertensive CKD with ESRD (I12.0). Anemia in CKD (D63.1).

Plan: Order duplex ultrasound of AV fistula to assess flow and outflow stenosis. Review recent labs and dialysis run sheets. Discussed contingency planning for alternative access if fistula remains unreliable, including tunneled dialysis catheter placement (CPT 36558) for durable access. Continue antihypertensives and ESA per nephrology.

224.bio_narrative: Ross Geller is a 57-year-old Caucasian male living in downtown San Antonio, Texas, who has been managing end stage renal disease (N18.6) with dependence on maintenance hemodialysis (Z99.2) for several years. He receives dialysis through a surgically created arteriovenous fistula (created 06-20-2023) and has remained consistent with scheduled sessions. Over time, he has continued to experience fatigue and “crash” symptoms on dialysis days, with intermittent muscle cramping and occasional post-dialysis lightheadedness. His chronic kidney disease has been accompanied by anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1), treated with epoetin alfa administered through the dialysis unit, and renal mineral-bone disorder characterized by secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81) and abnormal phosphorus metabolism (E83.39), managed with cinacalcet and sevelamer carbonate alongside strict dietary counseling. In early 2026, his care team at Houston Methodist Hospital intensified pre-transplant planning. A renal ultrasound on 01-03-2026 documented bilaterally small echogenic kidneys with cortical thinning consistent with chronic medical renal disease, providing baseline anatomic informatio ... (truncated)

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 57-year-old male with ESRD (N18.6) on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2) presents for regular follow-up focused on dialysis effectiveness and medication review. Reports ongoing fatigue and occasional muscle cramps after dialysis sessions. Denies fever, chest pain, or shortness of breath at rest. States he has been taking sevelamer with meals consistently and cinacalcet daily; epoetin alfa injections are administered through dialysis as scheduled.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and intermittent post-dialysis lightheadedness. Negative for fever/chills, cough, chest pain, GI bleeding symptoms, and focal neurologic deficits.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing, no acute distress. Regular rate/rhythm; lungs clear bilaterally; abdomen soft and non-tender. AV fistula with palpable thrill; no erythema or drainage.

Assessment: ESRD on hemodialysis with ongoing anemia of CKD and renal mineral-bone disorder.

Plan: Continue current dialysis schedule and renal diet. Continue Renvela with meals, Sensipar daily, and Epogen through dialysis unit. Reviewed BMP and renal ultrasound; coordinate ongoing transplant evaluation.
observations: Patient reports high adherence to renal diet and medications.
AV fistula exam reassuring with thrill and bruit present.

226.bio_narrative: Ross Geller is a 39-year-old male living in Houston, Texas who works in museum and field-based roles that sometimes require travel and irregular meals. Over the past several months he has experienced intermittent right-sided flank discomfort with urinary hesitancy and a reduced urinary stream. Symptoms are often accompanied by intermittent nausea and occasional vomiting (R11.2), especially during pain flares, which has affected his ability to concentrate at work and maintain his usual routine. He denies tobacco and illicit drug use and drinks alcohol minimally in social settings on weekends. He routinely walks for exercise and uses relaxation techniques when anxious during medical visits. He first sought specialty evaluation on 01-20-2026 for persistent flank pain and urinary hesitancy (R10.9, R39.11) with concern for obstruction and hydronephrosis. The working diagnoses include hydronephrosis with renal/ureteral calculous obstruction (N13.2) and unspecified hydronephrosis (N13.30). A blood test was obtained on 01-15-2026 as part of the initial workup. Medical therapy was initiated/continued with tamsulosin to facilitate stone passage, furosemide to manage perceived volume issues a ... (truncated)

He initially presented on 01-05-2026 describing constant aching even at rest, and has returned for follow-up visits due to ongoing symptoms and difficulty achieving consistent relief. Because of gastrointestinal sensitivity while using NSAIDs, he takes omeprazole daily for gastric protection. He reports completing a short course of physical therapy earlier in 2026 focused on shoulder mobility and strengthening, but he continues to report pain and functional limitation. images.findings: Five lumbar-type vertebrae. Mild disc space narrowing at L5-S1 with small anterior osteophytes. Alignment is maintained. No spondylolisthesis. Flexion/extension views show no abnormal translational movement to suggest dynamic instability. No acute fracture or destructive osseous lesion identified.

L5-S1: Right paracentral disc protrusion with annular fissure contacting the descending right S1 nerve root in the lateral recess; mild-to-moderate right neural foraminal narrowing. L4-L5: Mild broad-based disc bulge without high-grade stenosis. No significant central canal stenosis. Conus terminates normally. No epidural collection. Findings support targeted image-guided planning for the requested CT guidance for needle placement at the symptomatic level.

265.bio_narrative: Lisa Cuddy is a 57-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas, who is highly structured and goal-oriented and often pushes through symptoms until they affect her ability to function. Over the past several months she developed frequent heartburn and persistent epigastric pain (K21.9, R10.13). By early February 2026, she noticed intermittent dysphagia that felt like solid food was “getting stuck” (R13.10). She implemented lifestyle changes (earlier dinners, head-of-bed elevation), but her symptoms persisted and she escalated therapy with pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily. Despite adherence to the PPI trial, by March 2026 her dysphagia progressed and she began to feel exhausted. She reported darker stools concerning for melena (K92.1) and laboratory evaluation demonstrated iron deficiency anemia (D50.9), prompting iron supplementation and avoidance of NSAIDs. A CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast on 03-12-2026 did not identify an acute mass or obstruction, but her symptoms continued, and the limitation of CT for mucosal disease was discussed. A fecal immunochemical test was positive (R19.5), further supporting evaluation for a GI bleeding source. She also had confirmed Helicobacter pylori infection (A04.8), adding to concern for gastritis or peptic ulcer disease and the need for biopsy correlation. Her family history is notable for a father with peptic ulcer disease and a maternal aunt with gastrointestinal cancer in later adulthood, heightening her concern given alarm features. Socially, she works long hours in a demanding administrative role, does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally (1–2 drinks on weekends), and denies illicit drug use. She has a supportive friend network in Austin and is meticulous about medication adherence and symptom tracking. After gastroenterology consultation on 03-21-2026, she was started on sucralfate for mucosal protection while awaiting endoscopy. Given progressive dysphagia (R13.10), unintentional weight loss (R63.4), persistent epigastric pain/GERD (R10.13, K21.9) despite acid suppression, and objective anemia with suspected GI blood loss (D50.9, K92.1) plus confirmed H. pylori (A04.8), an upper GI endoscopy with biopsy (CPT 43239) was requested to directly visualize and sample the upper GI mucosa and guide next-step management.

encounters.doctor_note:

Over the past few weeks she has noticed intermittent dysphagia, especially with breads/meats, described as a sensation of food getting stuck (R13.10). She has tried dietary modifications and OTC famotidine with incomplete relief.

ROS: Positive for reflux, epigastric pain, intermittent nausea; negative for hematemesis. She notes occasional dark stools but is unsure; denies fever.

Assessment/Plan: GERD with persistent symptoms and epigastric pain; dysphagia warrants further workup. Start/continue pantoprazole 40 mg BID before meals; counsel on GERD lifestyle (avoid late meals, elevate head of bed). Avoid NSAIDs given possible dark stools; use acetaminophen PRN. Order baseline labs if symptoms persist and consider GI referral if dysphagia continues.

275.bio_narrative: Sansa Baratheon is a 70-year-old widowed woman living independently in Houston, Texas. She is soft-spoken, highly self-controlled in clinical visits, and tends to understate symptoms unless specifically asked. Over the last several months she has noticed intermittent upper abdominal discomfort that she describes as “indigestion” along with occasional nausea. The discomfort is typically epigastric (R10.13), comes and goes without a clear pattern, and is sometimes accompanied by a mild burning sensation she calls “heartburn,” consistent with a possible reflux component (K21.9). She has not reported vomiting, and her record does not document alarm symptoms such as hematemesis or melena. She has not tracked weight changes carefully and does not emphasize constitutional symptoms unless prompted. Her first contact for these symptoms was a telehealth visit in December 2025, where she described vague dyspepsia (K30) and occasional nausea (R11.0). She was advised to try dietary adjustments and intermittent OTC calcium carbonate antacid and to follow up if symptoms persisted. 274.bio_narrative: Ann Perkins is a 69-year-old divorced woman living in Austin, Texas, who is known for being highly organized and proactive about preventive care. She previously worked in public-facing administrative roles and remains active in her community, often coordinating appointments and health tasks for friends and family. Over the past two months, she developed frequent epigastric burning discomfort with reflux symptoms, including sour taste and regurgitation, worse with coffee and late meals. She also noticed fatigue and reduced stamina during her usual walks. She denied hematemesis and did not clearly observe melena, but her primary care physician obtained stool testing due to concern for occult blood loss. On 2026-03-12, her FIT/FOBT returned positive, documented as R19.5 (Other fecal abnormalities). A follow-up laboratory evaluation on 2026-03-14 demonstrated microcytic anemia with low ferritin and low transferrin saturation, consistent with D50.9 (Iron deficiency anemia, unspecified). Her symptoms persisted despite initiation of pantoprazole and focused lifestyle measures. A key contributing risk factor is her long-term NSAID exposure (Z79.1) related to chronic right knee osteoarthrit ... (truncated)

He recently established care with a new primary care physician after relocating within the city to be closer to a small support network. He is meticulous with routines and tends to self-manage minor symptoms with over-the-counter remedies before seeking medical advice. Over the past several months, he has noticed intermittent changes in bowel habits (R19.4) characterized by episodic constipation (K59.00) and variable stool frequency. He describes periods of one to two days without a bowel movement followed by smaller, less satisfying stools, without a clearly identified dietary trigger. Initial primary care evaluation focused on general counseling regarding hydration and fiber intake, with intermittent polyethylene glycol use discussed; no defined course of a structured bowel regimen is documented. 272.bio_narrative: Monica Geller is a 61-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who is meticulous about routines and tends to push through symptoms until they disrupt her day. Over the past 5–6 months she noticed a persistent change in bowel habits (R19.4), alternating between constipation with straining and episodes of looser stools. During this period she also began seeing intermittent dark stools and occasional red blood mixed with stool (K92.1). She initially attributed symptoms to stress and diet changes, but over time she developed progressive fatigue and an unintentional weight loss (R63.4), prompting her to seek care. At her primary care visit on 2026-02-18, she described ongoing cramping and bloating with intermittent bleeding. Conservative measures were started, including a bowel regimen with polyethylene glycol and diet counseling, and she used a short course of rectal hydrocortisone for suspected hemorrhoidal irritation (past use). Given the persistence and pattern of bleeding, her PCP ordered a fecal immunochemical test and laboratory evaluation. 266.bio_narrative: Roman Wambsgans is a 38-year-old male living in downtown Austin, Texas, who works in a high-demand professional role with frequent deadlines and travel. Over the past several months he has developed a bothersome change in bowel habit (R19.4) characterized by fluctuating stool consistency and frequency with intermittent urgency and crampy lower abdominal discomfort. He has also described episodic upper abdominal “unsettled” sensations consistent with functional dyspepsia (K30) that he manages intermittently with famotidine at bedtime. While he denies overt rectal bleeding and has not had documented prior GI endoscopy, the unpredictability of symptoms has impacted his concentration and planning during workdays and has contributed to increased health-related worry. His medical history is notable for generalized anxiety disorder (F41.1), treated with daily sertraline and ongoing cognitive-behavioral therapy every two weeks. Anxiety has tended to manifest as rumination and repeated checking behaviors, particularly when he perceives bodily changes. He also has a history of elevated blood pressure readings without a formal diagnosis of hypertension (R03.0), and he remains on amlodipine for blood pressure control. Socially, he denies tobacco and illicit drug use and drinks alcohol socially. His diet is variable with frequent restaurant meals and high caffeine intake; exercise is sporadic and typically limited to weekends. A stepwise evaluation began with a primary care visit on 03-04-2026 for bowel changes and abdominal unease, followed by telehealth follow-up on 03-18-2026 due to persistent symptoms and worry. Noninvasive workup on 03-15-2026 included CBC/CMP/TSH without anemia or metabolic abnormality and a negative FIT/FOBT. A limited abdominal ultrasound on 03-28-2026 did not show hepatobiliary pathology. He previously attempted a short trial of psyllium fiber and an OTC probiotic with inconsistent improvement. On 04-10-2026, he completed a gastroenterology specialist consultation and requested a definitive evaluation; diagnostic colonoscopy (CPT 45378) was planned to directly assess for colonic mucosal or structural causes of R19.4 that are not well characterized by prior noninvasive testing. A pre-op evaluation on 04-24-2026 reviewed medication holds, including avoidance of ibuprofen beginning 05-01-2026, and reinforced preparation steps with attention to anxiety management and blood pressure monitoring.

Patient with persistent dyspepsia/epigastric burning and intermittent nausea despite reported conservative measures and acid suppression use. EGD with biopsy is requested to evaluate for mucosal disease and guide management. Comorbidity: patient avoids NSAIDs due to prior GI upset and is in active psychotherapy for stress which correlates with symptom flares.

Objective evidence: 03-10-2026: Creatinine 0.84 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, CO2 26 mmol/L, Potassium 4.1 mmol/L, Hgb 13.4 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: K21.9 - Gastro-esophageal reflux disease without esophagitis
pa_request.previous_treatments: Famotidine PRN, calcium carbonate PRN, reflux precautions/diet modification, probiotic trial; ondansetron PRN for nausea; avoidance of NSAIDs due to dyspepsia history.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Diagnostic clarification via endoscopic visualization and biopsy to direct ongoing treatment planning.

264.bio_narrative: Carmela S. is a 59-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas who established care with Dr. Marisol Vega, MD at UT Southwestern Family Medicine at Park Cities for routine health maintenance and intermittent upper abdominal discomfort. She works in a family-owned retail business and often eats restaurant or convenience meals due to schedule demands. Over the past year, she has noticed recurrent post-prandial epigastric burning and dyspepsia (K30, R10.13) that is worse with coffee, larger portions, and late-night eating. She describes intermittent nausea (R11.0) during symptom flares and occasional reflux symptoms (K21.9). She tends to delay using PRN symptom-relief medications until discomfort becomes noticeable and reports variable adherence to dietary changes. Her symptoms first prompted a telehealth visit on 10-07-2025 for burning after meals. She was advised on reflux precautions and began using famotidine 20 mg at bedtime as needed along with calcium carbonate chews as needed. The symptoms recurred, leading to an in-person office visit on 02-18-2026 where she reported ongoing episodes and was prescribed ondansetron ODT PRN for nausea. She avoids NSAIDs after prior ibuprofen use worsened upper GI upset and relies on acetaminophen for occasional musculoskeletal pain. She also started a patient-directed probiotic trial. Despite these measures, she continued to experience meal-related discomfort that she reports interferes with her daily routine, including reduced appetite during flares and avoiding social meals; no standardized functional scoring instruments are documented. Family history is notable for relatives with reflux symptoms and diet-related GI complaints per patient report. Socially, she denies illicit drug use, drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends, and reports difficulty limiting caffeine and late-night eating. She is engaged in psychotherapy every two weeks with Dr. Nina Romano, PhD for stress management and recognizes stress as a trigger for symptom flares. A GI specialist consultation on 03-06-2026 reviewed her persistent dyspepsia/epigastric pain and intermittent nausea. As part of the diagnostic pathway prior to the requested Upper GI Endoscopy with Biopsy (EGD) (CPT 43239), she completed CBC/CMP testing on 03-10-2026 and a limited abdominal ultrasound on 03-14-2026, which did not show acute biliary abnormalities. Given the recurrent symptoms over months, reported impact on daily functioning, and noninvasive workup without an alternative explanation, EGD with biopsy was planned at Baylor University Medical Center Endoscopy Center to evaluate for mucosal disease and guide ongoing management.

267.bio_narrative: Anthony Stark is a 64-year-old male living in Houston, Texas, followed by Maya Hernandez, MD at Houston Methodist Primary Care Group – River Oaks. He has a history of essential hypertension (I10) controlled on amlodipine and losartan-hydrochlorothiazide and gastro-esophageal reflux disease without esophagitis (K21.9) that remains controlled on omeprazole. Over the past several months he has developed intermittent bright red blood per rectum (K62.5) accompanied by a noticeable change in bowel habits (R19.4), describing alternating harder stools and looser stools with intermittent urgency and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. He also notes episodic left lower quadrant discomfort (R10.32) that tends to coincide with periods of constipation or increased straining. He avoids NSAIDs during the bleeding evaluation and uses acetaminophen for intermittent discomfort. His colorectal risk profile is elevated due to a prior colonoscopy with polypectomy completed on 04-19-2022 with a documented personal history of colonic polyps (Z86.010). He also reports a high-risk family history of colorectal cancer (Z80.0), which increased his concern when bleeding recurred. After his 03-04-2026 primary care visit for hematochezia and stool changes, his bowel regimen was optimized with daily psyllium, hydration counseling, and short trials of polyethylene glycol and docusate (both later discontinued due to limited benefit). Baseline labs (CBC and CMP on 04-12-2026) were obtained to evaluate for anemia and to establish metabolic readiness for sedation and bowel preparation. On follow-up, a fecal immunochemical test returned positive on 04-15-2026 (R19.5), prompting escalation from conservative measures and stool-based assessment to direct mucosal evaluation. He was evaluated by gastroenterology (Aisha Patel, MD) on 03-18-2026 and again in pre-procedure review on 04-18-2026, where the timeline of symptoms, positive FIT, and prior polyp history were reviewed and diagnostic colonoscopy was ordered (CPT 45378). His social history includes former tobacco use with long-term abstinence, social alcohol use on weekends, and denial of illicit drug use. He works in a high-demand role and reports mild situational stress but no active depression or anxiety symptoms on screening. He has increased fiber and water intake and walks briskly 2–3 times per week. The current referral for diagnostic colonoscopy is intended to localize the source of bleeding, evaluate for recurrent adenomas or malignancy given Z86.010 and Z80.0, and guide next management and surveillance planning in the context of persistent symptoms (K62.5, R19.4) and objective stool test positivity (R19.5).

Review of Systems: GI positive for hematochezia and bowel habit change; negative for persistent melena. Constitutional negative for fever. CV/Resp negative for exertional chest pain or dyspnea.

Physical Exam: Well appearing; abdomen soft with mild LLQ tenderness; no guarding. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable.

Assessment: K62.5 and R19.4 in patient with Z86.010 and Z80.0. Plan to obtain FIT (R19.5 if positive) and proceed with diagnostic colonoscopy (CPT 45378) due to symptoms and risk factors; comorbid I10 and K21.9 stable.

Plan: Schedule colonoscopy at Houston Methodist Hospital Endoscopy Center; provide counseling on bowel prep importance, diet modification, and medication reconciliation. Continue fiber; maintain PPI for GERD control. Labs from PCP (CBC/CMP) to be reviewed when available.
observations: High-risk colorectal profile due to personal and family history.
Symptoms persistent despite conservative measures.

Assessment: K62.5, R19.4 with positive FIT (R19.5) and history Z86.010/Z80.0. Comorbid I10/K21.9 stable and relevant for procedural planning.

Plan: Reinforce GI plan for diagnostic colonoscopy (CPT 45378). Review CBC/CMP from 04-12-2026 as baseline; no urgent abnormalities noted. Continue psyllium and hydration; continue acetaminophen PRN and avoid NSAIDs. Provide education on what to expect with endoscopy scheduling and need for driver after sedation.

No signs of hemodynamic compromise at visit.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient presents for pre-procedure evaluation prior to requested diagnostic colonoscopy (CPT 45378) for intermittent hematochezia (K62.5), positive FIT (R19.5), and change in bowel habits (R19.4) with increased risk due to prior polyps (Z86.010) and family history (Z80.0). He reports stable symptoms without heavy bleeding since last visit and denies syncope. He has controlled hypertension (I10) and controlled GERD (K21.9) on omeprazole.

Review of Systems: Negative for chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations. GI positive for intermittent blood in stool; negative for severe abdominal pain today. No fevers.

Physical Exam: General well appearing. Heart regular rhythm; lungs clear. Abdomen soft, nontender today. No peripheral edema. Airway exam: Mallampati II, full neck ROM.

Assessment: Patient appropriate for planned sedation with attention to I10 and K21.9. Indications for diagnostic colonoscopy remain supported by K62.5, R19.5, R19.4 and high-risk history Z86.010, Z80.0.

Plan: Reviewed bowel prep and provided Golytely split-dose prescription; emphasized adherence for adequate visualization. Reviewed medication reconciliation: continue amlodipine and omeprazole as usual unless instructed by anesthesia; take morning dose of amlodipine with small sip of water on procedure day. Confirm diuretic component of losartan/HCTZ with pre-op nurse the day before; patient advised to follow endoscopy center protocol. ECG obtained for baseline due to age and hypertension (CPT 93000).
observations: Vitals stable; no active cardiopulmonary symptoms.
Patient understands bowel prep and sedation requirements.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Recurrent exertional chest pressure and dyspnea (R07.9, R06.02) in the setting of hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and obesity (I10, E11.9, E78.5, E66.9). Symptoms have progressed over several weeks and evaluation is time-sensitive to define ischemic burden and guide next steps. Myocardial perfusion imaging SPECT (CPT 78452) is requested to assess inducible ischemia and myocardial perfusion abnormalities to inform management.

Objective evidence: 04-09-2026: Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, eGFR 80 mL/min/1.73m²), BUN 17 mg/dL, CO2 25 mmol/L, Potassium 4.2 mmol/L | 03-18-2026: Creatinine 1.0, Hgb 14.0.
supporting_diagnoses: E11.9 - Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications
pa_request.previous_treatments: Conservative management including pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic approaches such as medication optimization (metoprolol, amlodipine), aspirin therapy, PRN nitroglycerin, and lifestyle counseling; activity modification due to knee osteoarthritis.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Define presence/extent of inducible ischemia and perfusion defects to guide cardiology management and risk stratification.

210.bio_narrative: Oscar Martinez is a 69-year-old Hispanic male living in Dallas, Texas, who has been followed by Dr. Karen Fields, MD at UT Southwestern Family Medicine for chronic cardiometabolic conditions including essential hypertension (I10), type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications (E11.9), hyperlipidemia (E78.5), and obesity (E66.9). He is methodical with self-monitoring and uses a pill organizer while logging blood pressure and fasting glucose several times per week. He lives with his spouse and remains independent in activities of daily living, though his routine walking has been limited by right knee osteoarthritis (M17.11). In late February 2026, he began experiencing intermittent substernal tightness described as “pressure” when climbing stairs or walking briskly, often accompanied by shortness of breath (R07.9, R06.02). These episodes generally last a few minutes and improve with rest. Because he has diabetes and dyslipidemia, he was concerned that symptoms could represent cardiac ischemia. At a 03-12-2026 primary care visit, his symptoms were reviewed and medical therapy for blood pressure and symptom control was optimized, with nitroglycerin prescribed for breakthrough episodes. On 03-18-2026 he presented to the ER with similar exertional chest tightness and dyspnea; laboratory testing and a chest X-ray were obtained without acute pulmonary findings, and he was discharged with close outpatient follow-up. He was evaluated by cardiology on 03-25-2026, where persistent exertional symptoms in the setting of multiple ASCVD risk factors were discussed. His knee osteoarthritis limits his ability to perform prolonged treadmill exercise, which influenced the test planning. A transthoracic echocardiogram (CPT 93306) was completed on 04-01-2026 to document baseline cardiac structure and function prior to nuclear testing. He has attended supervised cardiac rehab in the past and continues short walks and home exercises as tolerated, but he reports variable adherence to exercise during knee pain flares. Family history is notable for a father with myocardial infarction in his early 60s and a mother with longstanding hypertension; one sibling has type 2 diabetes. Oscar is a former smoker and drinks alcohol socially (1–2 drinks on weekends). The current referral requests myocardial perfusion imaging SPECT (CPT 78452) to evaluate myocardial perfusion and inducible ischemia in the setting of ongoing exertional chest pressure and dyspnea, and to inform the next steps in cardiology management while accounting for his diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and obesity.

210.behavioral_notes: Patient is highly organized and generally adherent with daily medications, using a pill organizer and logging BP/fasting glucose several times per week. He is motivated to understand test results and asks detailed questions, sometimes becoming anxious when information is vague; he benefits from clear written instructions. Former smoker; drinks alcohol socially (1–2 drinks on weekends). No illicit drug use. Diet improved recently (lower sodium, fewer sugary beverages) but he reports stress-related snacking and inconsistent exercise due to knee pain; he has been using PT home exercises and short walks as tolerated.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 69-year-old male with HTN, T2DM, HLD, obesity presents with 2–3 weeks of exertional substernal chest pressure described as tightness when climbing stairs or walking fast. Episodes last a few minutes, improve with rest, and are associated with shortness of breath. No syncope. He is anxious about cardiac causes given risk factors. 

Review of Systems: Positive for exertional chest pressure and exertional dyspnea. Negative for fever, cough, hemoptysis, leg swelling, or focal neurologic deficits. 

Physical Exam: BP elevated. Cardiovascular regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs, no edema. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender. 

Assessment: Exertional chest pain/pressure (R07.9) and shortness of breath (R06.02) in patient with risk factors (I10, E11.9, E78.5, E66.9). 

Plan: Start/continue metoprolol succinate ER for BP and symptom control and continue amlodipine. Prescribed nitroglycerin SL PRN and reviewed ER precautions. Continue statin and metformin. Ordered baseline labs (CBC/CMP with GGT) already drawn 03-09-2026; reviewed results. Referred to cardiology for further evaluation and discussion of myocardial perfusion imaging SPECT (CPT 78452) following specialist assessment.
observations: Patient appears anxious but cooperative; brings detailed home BP and glucose logs.
Exertional symptoms reproducible by history; resting exam unremarkable.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Recurrent exertional chest tightness and dyspnea persist despite medical therapy and completed initial testing (ECG and exercise treadmill stress test) with symptom reproduction. SPECT myocardial perfusion imaging is requested to objectively assess stress/rest perfusion and support risk stratification in the setting of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and obesity.

pa_request.expected_outcome: Perfusion-based assessment of inducible ischemia and functional risk stratification to guide ongoing medical management and any next diagnostic steps based on results.

211.bio_narrative: Maxine Porter is a 66-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who works long shifts in a busy service setting that often requires her to move quickly, carry supplies, and climb stairs. Over the last several weeks she has experienced recurrent episodes of chest tightness described as a pressure or “tight band” sensation that occurs primarily with exertion—rushing between tasks, carrying items, or climbing stairs—and is accompanied by shortness of breath. The episodes typically improve with rest; on 03-24-2026 one episode lasted longer than usual and prompted an ER visit. She reports the symptoms are anxiety-provoking when they occur at work, and she has been cautious about exertion since the episodes began. Her cardiovascular risk profile includes essential hypertension (I10), hyperlipidemia (E78.5), and obesity (E66.9). She does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely (about 1–2 drinks per month), and denies illicit drug use. Diet has been inconsistent due to late shifts and frequent reliance on high-sodium diner food, though she has started packing meals twice weekly. She is generally adherent with medications using a pill organizer but occasionally misses an evening dose during double shifts. She has ongoing mental health support (active psychotherapy every 2 weeks) and practices breathing exercises for stress management. Maxine’s family history is notable for premature coronary disease in her father (myocardial infarction in his early 60s) and hyperlipidemia in multiple first-degree relatives, increasing concern for ischemic etiologies in the setting of her exertional symptoms. On 03-11-2026 she was evaluated in primary care, where a 12-lead ECG (CPT 93000) was obtained as part of the initial workup of exertional chest tightness (R07.89) and dyspnea (R06.02). After the ER visit on 03-24-2026, a chest X-ray showed no acute cardiopulmonary process, supporting continued outpatient cardiac evaluation. Cardiology consultation on 04-08-2026 focused on persistent exertional symptoms and optimization of medical therapy; she continued antihypertensive therapy and was provided PRN nitroglycerin guidance. On 04-15-2026 she completed an exercise treadmill stress test (CPT 93015) that reproduced symptoms but did not fully define ischemic burden. Given ongoing exertional chest pain and dyspnea with risk factors, myocardial perfusion imaging with SPECT (CPT 78452) is scheduled for 05-20-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital to assess stress/rest perfusion and support risk stratification and treatment planning.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 66-year-old female with known hypertension reports several weeks of intermittent exertional chest tightness described as a pressure sensation, typically when rushing at work or climbing stairs, associated with shortness of breath. Episodes last 5–10 minutes and improve with rest. No syncope. She notes increased stress when symptoms occur. Tried avoiding heavy meals without clear improvement.

Review of Systems: Positive for exertional chest tightness (R07.89) and dyspnea (R06.02). Denies fever, productive cough, pleuritic pain, leg swelling, or recent immobilization. Denies melena or hematochezia. 

Physical Exam: Well-appearing, no acute distress. Cardiovascular: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or rales. No lower extremity edema.

Assessment: Exertional chest tightness and dyspnea with cardiovascular risk factors. Essential hypertension (I10) with above-goal BP today. Consider reflux contribution given meal timing but exertional pattern raises concern for cardiac etiology.

Plan: Performed 12-lead ECG (CPT 93000) today. Started trial of omeprazole (past course planned) for possible reflux component. Reinforced low-sodium diet and home BP monitoring. Advised ER precautions for prolonged/worsening symptoms. Referred to cardiology for further evaluation and stress testing pathway.

Plan: Schedule SPECT MPI (CPT 78452) for 05-20-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital Nuclear Cardiology. Reviewed pre-test instructions: no caffeine for 24 hours prior; fasting after midnight except sips of water for essential morning meds as directed. 220.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 69-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas, with long-standing type 2 diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease (E11.22) and hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0) that has progressed to end stage renal disease (N18.6). Over the past year she has required increasingly intensive medical management of ESRD complications, including phosphate binding for disordered phosphorus metabolism (E83.39), potassium-lowering therapy for recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5), and sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis (E87.2). She also carries anemia of chronic kidney disease (D63.1) treated with oral iron supplementation. She reports taking her scheduled medications consistently, though nausea and fatigue sometimes cause missed meals and shift medication timing; she uses a simple checklist to keep her regimen organized. Her current episode of care began with worsening uremic symptom burden in early 2026. At an office visit on 02-18-2026, she described persistent nausea most days and profound fatigue that reduced her activity level and made it difficult to maintain regular meals. On 03-10-2026 she presented to the emergency department for weakness and ongoing nausea, where laboratory testing reflected advanced kidney failure physiology with metabolic and electrolyte abnormalities, prompting expedited outpatient planning for renal replacement therapy. Subsequent outpatient labs on 03-22-2026 were used to trend anemia parameters (D63.1) and guide ongoing management of potassium, bicarbonate, and phosphorus abnormalities (E87.5, E87.2, E83.39). Renal ultrasound on 03-18-2026 demonstrated chronic medical renal disease changes consistent with ESRD (N18.6) without an obstructive cause. She completed upper extremity vascular mapping on 03-25-2026 to support dialysis access planning, and pre-dialysis cardiopulmonary baseline testing (12-lead ECG and chest x-ray) was obtained on 04-08-2026. A hepatitis B serology panel on 04-09-2026 and a catch-up hepatitis B vaccination on 03-12-2026 were completed in preparation for dialysis unit requirements. Socially, April is a former smoker with rare alcohol use and no illicit drug use. She lives with her partner, who helps with transportation to appointments. Her diet historically relied heavily on packaged convenience foods, and she is actively engaged with renal diet counseling every two weeks to implement structured, repeatable meal plans that fit her energy level and restrictions. Family history is notable for diabetes and hypertension in first-degree relatives, which aligns with her disease trajectory. She tends to delay care until symptoms disrupt routine, and her affect is guarded and skeptical; recent screening suggests mild anxiety centered on medical scheduling rather than persistent depression. The current referral focuses on coordinating initiation of dialysis services (CPT 90999) for ESRD (N18.6) with a planned start date and monitoring plan that accounts for her comorbid diabetes and hypertensive CKD and her documented metabolic complications.

images.findings: Ultrasound demonstrates bilaterally small echogenic kidneys with cortical thinning consistent with chronic medical renal disease in the setting of ESRD (N18.6). No hydronephrosis or obstructing stone is identified, supporting that symptoms and lab abnormalities are not due to an acute obstructive process. Bladder partially distended without focal mass. Findings were obtained as part of pre-dialysis diagnostic workup prior to requested dialysis services (CPT 90999).

images.facility: Ascension Seton Medical Center Austin - Vascular Lab
images.findings: Bilateral upper extremity mapping performed for dialysis access planning. Cephalic and basilic veins are patent with compressibility and no evidence of acute thrombosis; left cephalic vein caliber measured approximately 2.6–3.1 mm in the forearm and 3.2–3.6 mm in the upper arm, suitable for fistula consideration. Radial and brachial arteries demonstrate triphasic flow without significant stenosis. Study obtained specifically to support access planning prior to initiation of requested dialysis (CPT 90999).

images.facility: Ascension Seton Medical Center Austin - Radiology
images.findings: Two-view chest radiograph obtained as baseline cardiopulmonary assessment prior to planned dialysis initiation (CPT 90999). Cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal size limits. No focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax is identified. Mild vascular prominence without frank pulmonary edema; correlate with volume status in ESRD (N18.6) and hypertensive CKD (I12.0).

reports.results: Key abnormalities consistent with advanced kidney failure physiology: elevated BUN and creatinine with reduced estimated renal clearance in the setting of ESRD (N18.6). Potassium was elevated compared with reference range, consistent with hyperkalemia risk (E87.5), and serum bicarbonate was low consistent with metabolic acidosis (E87.2). CBC showed anemia pattern consistent with anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1). Point-of-care glucose elevated, consistent with diabetes with diabetic CKD (E11.22).
reports.notes: Labs obtained during ER visit for weakness and nausea to evaluate metabolic derangements prior to dialysis planning (CPT 90999). Results were reviewed with patient and used to recommend expedited outpatient follow-up for renal replacement therapy planning and continued potassium and bicarbonate management.

reports.results: Chemistry panel demonstrated persistent renal failure values consistent with ESRD (N18.6), with bicarbonate remaining below goal supporting ongoing acidosis management (E87.2) and potassium trending above goal supporting continued hyperkalemia management (E87.5). Phosphorus was elevated with abnormal mineral parameters consistent with disorders of phosphorus metabolism (E83.39) and ongoing need for phosphate binder therapy. CBC showed chronic anemia consistent with anemia in CKD (D63.1); iron studies supported continued oral iron supplementation as part of anemia support strategy.
reports.notes: Outpatient monitoring labs ordered to guide medication adjustments and to provide objective baseline values in preparation for planned dialysis initiation (CPT 90999). Findings were discussed at subsequent visits and incorporated into access and dialysis planning steps.

reports.results: Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) negative; hepatitis B core antibody (total) negative; hepatitis B surface antibody (anti-HBs) low/negative, consistent with non-immune status at time of testing and supporting catch-up vaccination documented on 03-12-2026. No serologic evidence of active hepatitis B infection was identified.
reports.notes: Serology obtained to support outpatient dialysis unit infection-control requirements in preparation for requested dialysis services (CPT 90999). Results documented for dialysis placement coordination and vaccine follow-up planning.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Persistent functional impairment from malocclusion with chewing fatigue and diet limitation, with associated dysphagia complaints and chronic mouth breathing. Le Fort I osteotomy (CPT 21141) requested to improve maxillary position and occlusal function for mastication and swallowing mechanics, with perioperative planning informed by obesity and suspected OSA.

Objective evidence: 04-04-2026: Creatinine 0.92, BUN 18, CO2 26, Potassium 4.1 | 04-04-2026: Hgb 12.9.
supporting_diagnoses: J34.89 - Other specified disorders of nose and nasal sinuses
pa_request.previous_treatments: Conservative management including pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic approaches, with persistent functional limitations.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved occlusal function and chewing efficiency, decreased jaw fatigue with meals, improved diet texture tolerance, and improved coordination of swallowing mechanics with continued speech therapy.

230.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 68-year-old woman living in downtown Austin, Texas. She works in a desk-based municipal role and reports that her symptoms tend to build gradually until they interfere with daily routines. Over the past 12–18 months she has noticed increasing chewing inefficiency with a bite that “doesn’t line up,” leading to jaw fatigue halfway through meals and avoidance of foods that require prolonged mastication. She compensates by cutting food into small pieces and choosing softer textures, and she describes intermittent throat irritation with occasional sensation of difficulty initiating a swallow (R13.10), particularly when her mouth is dry. She also reports chronic nasal obstruction (J34.89) with habitual mouth breathing most days, waking with dry mouth, and loud snoring with non-restorative sleep; suspected obstructive sleep apnea (G47.33) is discussed in perioperative planning. She uses daily intranasal fluticasone and saline spray, and she takes omeprazole for reflux symptoms that contribute to throat irritation during dysphagia complaints. Jaw discomfort and masticatory muscle fatigue are managed with intermittent naproxen, with acetaminophen used as an alternative during preoperative NSAID hold planning. She has obesity (E66.9), and perioperative medication planning includes rivaroxaban considerations documented in the surgical pathway. A surgical-orthodontic approach was initiated with fixed appliance placement on 06-10-2025. Primary care evaluation on 07-29-2025 documented persistent nasal obstruction and functional chewing complaints. Oral and maxillofacial surgery consultation on 03-10-2026 documented Angle class II malocclusion (M26.212) with functional limitations in mastication and coordinated the workup for Le Fort I osteotomy (CPT 21141). Imaging and preoperative labs in early April 2026 were obtained to characterize maxillary anatomy and support anesthesia planning. She remains engaged in weekly speech therapy focused on compensatory strategies for swallowing comfort and oral-motor coordination. Socially, she does not use tobacco, drinks alcohol occasionally, and denies illicit drug use. She walks for light exercise but notes fatigue after nights of restless sleep. Family history is notable for a father with sleep apnea and a mother with chronic allergic rhinitis, which she associates with her own long-standing nasal symptoms. The current referral is for surgical correction of maxillary position to improve functional occlusion and chewing mechanics and to support safer swallowing with coordinated perioperative airway planning.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Persistent functional chewing impairment with jaw pain and masticatory fatigue is documented alongside cephalometric and CBCT evidence of skeletal maxillary discrepancy and malocclusion. Conservative and orthodontic measures have not sufficiently improved function, and orthognathic correction is planned with perioperative management of hypertension and anxiety.

Objective evidence: 04-18-2026: Creatinine 0.86, Potassium 4.1, Hgb 12.9.
pa_request.previous_treatments: Orthodontic fixed appliance therapy; occlusal guard; physical therapy; acetaminophen and topical diclofenac; past short course ibuprofen discontinued due to gastritis symptoms with ongoing omeprazole for gastric protection.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved occlusal stability and masticatory function with reduced chewing-related jaw pain and fatigue; improved dietary tolerance.

231.bio_narrative: Arya Sandoval is a 68-year-old woman living in Fort Worth, Texas, who has experienced a gradual worsening of bite instability and chewing-related jaw pain over the past year. She initially sought dental and medical attention after noticing that her teeth were not meeting evenly and that chewing tougher foods caused her jaw to ache and become tired by the end of the day. Over time, she began limiting food textures and taking smaller bites because of jaw pain (R68.84), temporomandibular disorder symptoms (M26.609), and a persistent sense that her bite “slides” into an uncomfortable position during closure. Her care has involved coordinated treatment between primary care, orthodontics, and oral and maxillofacial surgery. Orthodontic fixed appliances were placed on 08-05-2025 to address malocclusion and dental compensations, followed by ongoing adjustments. An occlusal guard was fabricated and fitted on 11-18-2025 to reduce nocturnal clenching and help unload masticatory muscles, but she continued to report chewing fatigue (R53.83) and end-of-day soreness. She has used acetaminophen and topical diclofenac for symptom control, and she avoids ibuprofen (past) after it triggered gastritis symptoms; she remains on omeprazole for gastric protection. Her hypertension (I10) is controlled with amlodipine, and she takes escitalopram for generalized anxiety disorder (F41.1), which is monitored because pain and sleep disruption worsen her stress. On 03-14-2026, she reported to her PCP that chewing makes her jaw ache and tire quickly. At a specialist consult on 04-01-2026, the OMFS team documented persistent functional limitation despite orthodontic therapy and suspected an underlying skeletal discrepancy. A cephalometric radiograph with analysis and CBCT of the maxillofacial skeleton were obtained on 04-22-2026 and reviewed the same day. Imaging documented a skeletal maxillary discrepancy consistent with maxillary hypoplasia (M26.12) and malocclusion, Angle class II (M26.212), correlating with her longstanding functional symptoms. Given the objective skeletal findings, ongoing chewing limitation, and persistence of symptoms despite conservative measures, she is scheduled for a requested Le Fort/Osteotomy (CPT 21141) with coordinated orthodontic planning. Socially, she is highly self-directed, keeps a written medication schedule, and tends to underreport pain until it affects daily routines. She walks regularly for exercise, avoids tobacco, uses alcohol occasionally, and denies illicit drug use. Family history is notable for relatives with hypertension and arthritis, which informs her attention to long-term function and pain coping strategies. Preoperative testing and evaluations were completed to support anesthesia planning, and she has been engaged in care planning with detailed questions regarding perioperative medication holds and postoperative diet progression.

222.procedure_requested: Insertion of tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis

procedure_facility.facility_name: Dell Seton Medical Center at The University of Texas

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.9) and CKD stage 4 (N18.4) has persistent fatigue (R53.83) and intermittent nausea (R11.0) with recent objective lab evaluation consistent with advanced renal dysfunction and anemia of CKD (D63.1). Tunneled dialysis catheter placement is requested to establish reliable hemodialysis access and to coordinate anticoagulation management (Z79.01) in a controlled setting, given prior difficult peripheral access. The goal is to ensure timely access for planned renal replacement therapy initiation and reduce repeated IV attempts.

Objective evidence: 04-17-2026: Creatinine 3.4 mg/dL, BUN 58 mg/dL, CO2 18 mmol/L, Potassium 5.2 mmol/L, Hgb 9.1 g/dL | 04-17-2026: CO2 650 mg.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.9 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 1 through stage 4 chronic kidney disease, or unspecified chronic kidney disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
Z79.01 - Long term (current) use of anticoagulants
pa_request.previous_treatments: Antihypertensive therapy (amlodipine), diuretic therapy (furosemide), oral bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis related to CKD, oral iron supplementation for anemia of CKD, PRN ondansetron for nausea. Dietary counseling visit completed; ongoing psychotherapy to support adherence and stress management. Past lisinopril trial discontinued due to renal function/hyperkalemia concern.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Reliable vascular access for planned hemodialysis initiation and improved coordination of peri-procedural and dialysis-related care.

222.bio_narrative: Dina Fox is a 60-year-old woman living in downtown Austin, Texas, who has been followed in primary care for long-standing hypertension complicated by hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.9). Over the past year she has experienced a gradual decline in renal function documented as chronic kidney disease stage 4 (N18.4), with increasing day-to-day symptom burden. Since early 2026, she reports persistent fatigue (R53.83) that limits her ability to tolerate long shifts, along with intermittent nausea (R11.0) that comes in waves and sometimes reduces her appetite and fluid intake. She uses a pill organizer and prefers clear, stepwise instructions, but acknowledges occasional missed doses on long workdays. Her course became more clinically pressing after an ER visit on 03-24-2026 for nausea and profound fatigue, where CBC/CMP supported anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) and advanced renal dysfunction consistent with her CKD stage. During that visit, staff documented difficult peripheral IV access, reinforcing the need for a reliable access plan as she transitions toward renal replacement therapy. Subsequent outpatient testing included a renal ultrasound on 03-31-2026 showing chronic medical renal disease without obstructive uropathy and a limited upper-extremity venous mapping study on 04-04-2026 to inform access planning. She remains on amlodipine and furosemide for blood pressure/volume management, sodium bicarbonate for CKD-associated metabolic acidosis, and ferrous sulfate for CKD-related anemia; ondansetron is used as needed for symptomatic nausea. A prior lisinopril trial was stopped due to concerns for worsening renal function and mild hyperkalemia. She also takes apixaban (Z79.01), which requires careful coordination around procedures. Socially, Dina works variable shifts that interfere with consistent diet adherence; she is attempting lower sodium choices but describes challenges when meals are rushed. She is a former smoker and reports rare alcohol use, denying illicit drug use. She reports elevated stress and irritability when her medical plan feels uncertain, and she is engaged in active psychotherapy every two weeks. Family history is notable for hypertension and kidney disease in a first-degree relative, which has shaped her concerns about progression. Given persistent symptoms, objective findings consistent with advanced CKD, and documented access challenges, she is scheduled for insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) on 06-02-2026 at Dell Seton Medical Center at The University of Texas to establish reliable hemodialysis access and support a planned transition to renal replacement therapy.

2221.procedure_requested: Insertion of tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with advanced chronic kidney disease and progressive symptoms consistent with uremic burden, in the setting of hypertensive chronic kidney disease and CKD-related anemia. Tunneled dialysis catheter placement is requested to establish central venous access for initiation of hemodialysis planning and to reduce risk of emergent temporary access placement.

Objective evidence: 04-09-2026: Creatinine 5.2, BUN 58, CO2 21, Potassium 4.8, Hgb 10.6 g/dL | 04-09-2026: CO2 21 mmol/L..
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Medical management of hypertensive CKD with amlodipine and carvedilol; CKD-related anemia support with oral iron and monitoring; phosphate binder (sevelamer) and vitamin D supplementation; symptom-directed antiemetic (ondansetron). Past intermittent diuretic use (furosemide) for swelling earlier in course.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Establish tunneled central venous access to support initiation and delivery of hemodialysis when started, improve reliability of treatments, and reduce the need for emergent non-tunneled catheter placement.

2221.bio_narrative: Amelia Shepherd is a 42-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas, followed by Dr. Owen Hunt, MD at UT Southwestern Medical Center for hypertensive chronic kidney disease. Over the last year she has experienced a gradual decline in stamina with persistent daily fatigue (R53.83) and intermittent nausea (R11.0) that she associates with worsening kidney function. She works long shifts and reports that symptoms are most noticeable at the end of the day, when she feels “drained” and has difficulty maintaining consistent meal patterns. She denies tobacco use and denies illicit drug use. She drinks alcohol occasionally, typically 1–2 drinks on some weekends. She attends psychotherapy every other week to manage stress and sleep disruption related to chronic illness. Her kidney disease course has included monitoring and supportive therapy for anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1), treated with oral ferrous sulfate on an every-other-day schedule, and CKD nutrition and mineral management with sevelamer and vitamin D supplementation. Blood pressure is treated with amlodipine and carvedilol for hypertensive CKD (I12.0). She uses ondansetron ODT as needed for nausea and acetaminophen as needed for headaches that occur during periods of poor sleep. She previously used furosemide as needed for swelling earlier in the course of CKD stage 5 (N18.5), which was later discontinued when edema became less prominent. A renal ultrasound was completed on 09-18-2025 as part of CKD progression evaluation. In March 2026 she established nephrology care for symptom progression and ongoing decline in kidney function, with labs and iron studies obtained on 03-25-2026 to support anemia and metabolic monitoring. At follow-up visits in late March and early April 2026, she continued to report fatigue and intermittent nausea and expressed concern about needing dialysis soon. She completed a pre-operative evaluation on 04-07-2026 and an access planning visit on 04-08-2026 to review dialysis access steps. Chest X-ray on 04-02-2026 was obtained as baseline cardiopulmonary assessment in preparation for the requested tunneled hemodialysis catheter placement (CPT 36558), with pre-procedure labs drawn on 04-09-2026 for procedural planning. Her family history is notable for hypertension in both parents and chronic kidney disease in her maternal grandmother. The current referral and request are focused on establishing tunneled central venous access for hemodialysis planning in the context of advanced CKD stage 5 and associated symptom burden.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: I’m exhausted all the time and my labs keep getting worse.
encounters.chief_complaint: I’m exhausted all the time and my labs keep getting worse.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 42-year-old female with hypertensive CKD reports worsening fatigue over several months with reduced ability to get through long work shifts. She notes intermittent nausea without persistent vomiting and occasional headaches when sleep-deprived. She denies fevers, chest pain, dyspnea at rest, or new swelling.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and intermittent nausea. Negative for cough, orthopnea, dysuria, hematuria, melena, focal weakness.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing, no acute distress. Lungs clear to auscultation. Heart regular rhythm. Abdomen soft, non-tender. No pitting edema.

Assessment: Advanced CKD stage 5 (N18.5) due to hypertensive CKD (I12.0) with anemia of CKD (D63.1) and symptom burden including nausea (R11.0) and fatigue (R53.83).

Plan: Order CBC/BMP and iron studies dated 03-25-2026; continue BP regimen (amlodipine, carvedilol). Continue oral iron and vitamin D. Start/continue ondansetron ODT PRN nausea. Discussed dialysis education and potential need for tunneled catheter (CPT 36558) if dialysis initiation approaches. Return in 4 weeks for repeat evaluation and decision on proceeding with dialysis access.
observations: Reports progressive fatigue impacting work tolerance.
Intermittent nausea without vomiting.
No edema on exam.
