113.procedure_requested: Insertion of tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) has recurrent access dysfunction (T82.590A) with repeated inability to complete prescribed treatments and documented symptom escalation including dyspnea/edema requiring ED care. Objective duplex ultrasound confirms hemodynamically significant stenosis with reduced access flow, and dialysis unit data show persistent high venous pressures and shortened runs. Tunneled catheter placement is requested to provide reliable dialysis access in the context of heart failure (I50.32) and hypertension (I10) while definitive access management is arranged.

Objective evidence: 04-15-2026: Creatinine 8.9, BUN 62, CO2 22, Potassium 5.2, Hgb 9.2.
supporting_diagnoses: T82.590A - Other mechanical complication of other cardiac and vascular devices and implants, initial encounter
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Dialysis unit access troubleshooting with repeated cannulation/flow adjustments; outpatient vascular duplex imaging; antiplatelet therapy during access dysfunction evaluation; prior AV fistula creation on 03-15-2022; short course Lokelma during prior hyperkalemia episode.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Reliable vascular access to support completion of prescribed hemodialysis sessions and improve delivery of dialysis/ultrafiltration goals in a volume-sensitive patient.

113.bio_narrative: Corrado Soprano is a 73-year-old man living in downtown Dallas, Texas, who has been managing end stage renal disease (N18.6) with thrice-weekly in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2). He previously transitioned from late-stage chronic kidney disease to dialysis after progressive fluid retention and blood pressure instability, and he underwent creation of a left radiocephalic arteriovenous fistula on 03-15-2022. For several years the fistula supported his routine treatments, but over the past two months he has experienced repeated dialysis machine alarms, elevated venous pressures, and shortened treatments that leave him fatigued and volume overloaded. He describes difficulty “finishing treatments like I used to,” with cramping and early termination of sessions, followed by worsening leg swelling and shortness of breath. The clinical course escalated in early April 2026 when he presented to the emergency department with dyspnea and peripheral edema after a shortened dialysis run. His evaluation included chest X-ray findings consistent with pulmonary vascular congestion and cardiomegaly, aligning with his known chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) and essential hypertension (I10). His dialysis team and nephrologist have repeatedly attempted access troubleshooting at the chairside, including needle repositioning and flow adjustments, but the pattern persisted and impacted delivered dialysis time. A duplex ultrasound of the left upper extremity access on 04-02-2026 demonstrated hemodynamically significant stenosis with reduced access flow, supporting a mechanical complication of vascular access (T82.590A). He also carries anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1), managed with epoetin alfa administered during dialysis. Socially, he is widowed and lives alone but has family support for transportation when needed; he values straightforward written instructions and tends to be cautious with new clinicians until plans are concrete. He is a former smoker and drinks alcohol only on special occasions. Diet adherence is variable around social events; he tries to limit sodium and potassium but acknowledges occasional lapses. Family history is notable for cardiovascular disease, which he connects to his own blood pressure and heart failure management. Given recurrent treatment interruptions, objective evidence of fistula dysfunction, and the clinical consequences of inadequate dialysis in a volume-sensitive patient, his nephrologist and interventional radiology team are planning insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) on 05-20-2026 to establish dependable access for ongoing hemodialysis while definitive access management is pursued.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of recurrent dialysis access alarms and shortened hemodialysis sessions in an ESRD patient on chronic dialysis; review of symptoms and next diagnostic steps.
encounters.chief_complaint: My dialysis access keeps alarming and I can't finish treatments like I used to.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 73-year-old male with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) via left radiocephalic AV fistula created 03-15-2022 presents with 4–6 weeks of recurrent access alarms and shortened dialysis runs. Reports rising venous pressures during treatment per dialysis staff, intermittent cramping, and post-dialysis fatigue when sessions end early. Denies fever or access site drainage. Notes mild increase in ankle swelling on days after shortened sessions.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and intermittent dyspnea on exertion, mild peripheral edema. Negative for chest pain, syncope, fever/chills, cough, focal neurologic deficits.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing, no acute distress. Lungs with faint bibasilar crackles. CV: regular rhythm. Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema. Left forearm AV fistula with palpable thrill, no erythema, warmth, or drainage; mild tenderness at cannulation sites without fluctuance.

Assessment: ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) with suspected access dysfunction/mechanical complication (T82.590A) based on repeated alarms and shortened runs. Hypertension (I10) and chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) with volume sensitivity. Anemia of CKD (D63.1) treated per dialysis protocol.

Plan: Order duplex ultrasound of hemodialysis access to assess for stenosis; coordinate vascular access evaluation. Continue dialysis schedule; counsel on low sodium intake and daily weights. Continue current medications including amlodipine and carvedilol for BP/HF control and dialysis meds (sevelamer, calcitriol, epoetin). Discussed that if access dysfunction persists and dialysis adequacy is compromised, temporary alternative access may be needed including tunneled catheter (CPT 36558) while definitive access management proceeds.
observations: Dialysis unit reports high venous pressure alarms and reduced delivered treatment time.
Left forearm AV fistula thrill present without overt infection signs.
Mild edema suggests incomplete ultrafiltration on shortened runs.

encounters.chief_complaint: I'm short of breath and my legs are swelling after my dialysis got cut short.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 73-year-old male with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) presents with worsening dyspnea and bilateral leg swelling. He reports today’s dialysis session was terminated early due to access alarms and high venous pressures, and he did not reach his usual ultrafiltration goal. Over the last 24–48 hours he has had increasing orthopnea and reduced exercise tolerance. No fever, no chest pain.

Review of Systems: Positive for shortness of breath, orthopnea, and edema. Negative for pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, fever/chills.

Physical Exam: Mild respiratory distress on arrival, speaking full sentences. Lungs with bibasilar crackles. CV: regular rhythm; no murmurs appreciated. 2+ pitting edema to mid-shins. AV fistula left forearm without erythema or drainage.

Assessment: Volume overload in the setting of ESRD with incomplete dialysis delivery (N18.6, Z99.2) and chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32). Known dialysis access dysfunction/mechanical complication (T82.590A) contributing to inadequate ultrafiltration. Hypertension (I10). Anemia of CKD (D63.1) by history.

Plan: Obtain chest X-ray to evaluate pulmonary edema; coordinate urgent hemodialysis session due to symptoms and hypoxia. Continue home antihypertensives as tolerated; monitor oxygenation and hemodynamics. Reinforce need for reliable dialysis access strategy given recurrent access failure; advised follow-up with nephrology and vascular access team for next steps including consideration of tunneled dialysis catheter (CPT 36558).
observations: Hypoxia and crackles consistent with fluid overload.
History of shortened dialysis due to access alarms.
Edema and hypertension likely worsened by inadequate ultrafiltration.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient returns after ED visit for dyspnea/edema following shortened dialysis. Reports ongoing issues at dialysis center with high venous pressure alarms and early termination of sessions, leading to residual swelling and fatigue. He brings dialysis unit notes documenting repeated shortened treatments. Denies fever or access site drainage.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and intermittent dyspnea when dialysis is incomplete; swelling improves after full runs. Negative for chest pain, fever.

Physical Exam: Comfortable at rest. Lungs with minimal crackles. 1–2+ bilateral lower extremity edema. Left AV fistula with thrill present; no erythema.

Assessment: ESRD on dialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) with ongoing access dysfunction (T82.590A) and demonstrated clinical consequences (volume overload) in setting of chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32). Hypertension (I10). Anemia in CKD (D63.1).

Plan: Reviewed duplex ultrasound results showing significant stenosis and reduced access flow. Due to recurrent inability to complete dialysis reliably and recent acute volume overload, proceed with expedited planning for tunneled dialysis catheter (CPT 36558) as reliable access bridge; refer to interventional radiology for evaluation and scheduling. Order pre-op CBC/BMP/coagulation and update medication list. Discussed catheter care and infection prevention. Patient prefers written plan; provided printed instructions and reviewed medication considerations with antiplatelet therapy.

Volume sensitivity with HF increases consequence of missed ultrafiltration.
No clinical evidence of access infection today.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 73-year-old male with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) scheduled for tunneled central venous catheter insertion for dialysis (CPT 36558) on 05-20-2026 due to recurrent access dysfunction (T82.590A) with repeated shortened treatments and recent ED presentation for volume overload. Patient reports no current fever or symptoms of infection. He notes improved breathing after full dialysis runs but recurring swelling when sessions are cut short.

Review of Systems: Positive for intermittent dyspnea with volume overload and edema; negative for chest pain, fever, productive cough.

Physical Exam: Alert, oriented, cooperative. Airway: Mallampati II. Lungs with mild bibasilar crackles. Heart regular rhythm. Extremities with 1+ edema. Left AV fistula without erythema.

Assessment: ESRD/dialysis dependence requiring reliable access (N18.6, Z99.2) with access mechanical complication (T82.590A). Comorbid chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) and hypertension (I10) raise risk from missed dialysis and influence peri-procedural volume management. Anemia of CKD (D63.1).

Plan: Reviewed pre-op labs (CBC/BMP/coags) from 04-15-2026. Medication instructions: Continue amlodipine and carvedilol the morning of procedure with sip of water unless otherwise directed; continue sevelamer/calcitriol/epoetin per dialysis protocol. Continue aspirin 81 mg daily. Hold clopidogrel starting 05-15-2026 for procedure on 05-20-2026 unless updated by IR/nephrology. Dialysis scheduling: recommend hemodialysis on 05-19-2026 to optimize volume and electrolytes. NPO instructions provided. Discussed procedural risks and need for catheter care post-placement.

Concurrent Care Reference: Patient completed medication management (psychiatry) therapy in the past; no active therapy sessions at this time.
observations: Medication reconciliation performed with emphasis on antiplatelet hold dates.
NPO and dialysis timing discussed for ESRD patients.
No signs of active infection; vitals stable.

images.type: Duplex Ultrasound - Left Upper Extremity Hemodialysis Access
images.findings: Patent left radiocephalic AV fistula with hemodynamically significant juxta-anastomotic stenosis and reduced access flow (approx. 380 mL/min). Post-stenotic turbulence noted with findings correlating with elevated venous pressures and repeated dialysis alarms. No pseudoaneurysm or perivascular fluid collection; central venous outflow waveforms show dampened phasicity suggesting outflow limitation contributing to recirculation risk and inadequate dialysis delivery.

Mild cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular congestion and mild interstitial edema; probable small bilateral pleural effusions. No focal consolidation and no pneumothorax. Imaging obtained during ED evaluation for dyspnea/edema after shortened dialysis; supports clinical impact of incomplete ultrafiltration in a patient with heart failure and ESRD.

reports.results: CBC: WBC 7.8, Hgb 9.2, Plt 214. BMP: Na 136, K 5.2, CO2 22, BUN 62, Cr 8.9, Glu 108, Ca 8.4. Coags: PT 12.6, INR 1.1, aPTT 31.
reports.notes: Anemia consistent with CKD (D63.1) in ESRD on dialysis (N18.6, Z99.2). Coagulation parameters acceptable for procedural planning; patient on aspirin and clopidogrel during access evaluation with planned clopidogrel hold prior to tunneled catheter insertion (CPT 36558). Mild hyperkalemia consistent with ESRD; dialysis timing planned pre-procedure.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with chronic kidney disease and hypertensive chronic kidney disease complicated by anemia and recurrent hyperkalemia risk has progressive, functionally limiting fatigue and cramping affecting daily activities. Tunneled dialysis catheter placement is requested to establish reliable central venous access for hemodialysis initiation/access planning and to avoid repeated urgent temporary access needs.

Objective evidence: 03-31-2026: Creatinine 3.1 mg/dL, BUN 58 mg/dL, CO2 20 mmol/L, Potassium 5.3 mmol/L, Hgb 9.4 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Conservative management including pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic approaches such as dietary modification counseling, medication optimization, potassium-binding therapy, and anemia management.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Establish reliable vascular access to support initiation of hemodialysis treatments and ongoing renal replacement therapy planning while monitoring CKD-related anemia and electrolyte disturbances.

112.bio_narrative: Jesse Pinkman is a 67-year-old male living in Dallas, Texas, followed primarily at UT Southwestern Internal Medicine Clinic by Dr. Daniel Kim, MD. Over the past year he has had gradually worsening fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance, initially presenting to primary care on 10-01-2025 with persistent tiredness and elevated blood pressures. He works part-time in a warehouse/receiving role and reports that by late afternoon he often feels “wiped out,” needing frequent breaks. He lives alone but has support from a close friend who serves as his emergency contact. In early February 2026, he presented to the emergency department with weakness and muscle cramping, prompting evaluation for electrolyte abnormalities and renal complications. Outpatient potassium-lowering therapy was started and he began paying closer attention to low-potassium guidance, though he acknowledges lapses when routines change. Subsequent nephrology consultation on 03-28-2026 focused on chronic kidney disease management, hypertensive chronic kidney disease, anemia in chronic kidney disease treated with epoetin and iron supplementation, and recurrent hyperkalemia risk requiring potassium binder therapy. He describes his current symptoms as significantly limiting, with decreased stamina for work tasks and home activities. As part of planning for renal replacement therapy access, a pre-operative evaluation on 04-04-2026 included duplex ultrasound mapping of the bilateral internal jugular and subclavian veins. Follow-up visits on 04-10-2026 and 04-15-2026 focused on coordinating labs and cardiopulmonary baseline testing (ECG and chest X-ray) in preparation for tunneled dialysis catheter placement. He continues to report severe fatigue and intermittent cramping despite ongoing medication management. Social history includes former cigarette smoking, social alcohol use on weekends, and denial of current illicit drug use. Diet remains inconsistent and often relies on processed foods, and he is trying to implement low-potassium choices with difficulty meal planning. Family history is notable for hypertension in his father and type 2 diabetes in his mother. The current referral request is for insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) to establish reliable access for hemodialysis initiation/access planning given ongoing CKD-related complications and functional decline.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of persistent fatigue and elevated blood pressure readings at home; review of kidney function history.
encounters.chief_complaint: I’m tired all the time and my blood pressure’s been high.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 67-year-old male presents with several months of fatigue and elevated home BP readings. Reports poor sleep quality and increased daytime tiredness. Denies chest pain, syncope, or focal neurologic deficits. Notes intermittent ankle swelling after long shifts. Has known chronic kidney disease and is concerned it is worsening.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and occasional mild headaches. Negative for fever, cough, dyspnea at rest, palpitations, hematuria, or dysuria. No nausea/vomiting.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing male, no acute distress. BP elevated. Cardiovascular: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs clear bilaterally. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Extremities: trace bilateral ankle edema. Neuro grossly intact.

Assessment: Hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0) with fatigue (R53.83). Discussed kidney-protective BP control and avoidance of NSAIDs.

Plan: Continue amlodipine; discontinue lisinopril given kidney function concerns and hyperkalemia risk. Encourage home BP log, low-sodium diet, hydration guidance within renal recommendations, and lab monitoring at next interval. Discussed anemia screening and referral to nephrology if decline continues.

116.procedure_requested: Injection procedure for nephrostogram and/or ureterogram

procedure_facility.facility_name: Baylor University Medical Center - Interventional Radiology

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with right percutaneous nephrostomy tube for hydronephrosis with obstructing calculus (N13.2) continues to have intermittent right flank/abdominal pain (R10.9) and variable nephrostomy output requiring anatomic assessment of collecting system and ureteral patency. CPT 50394 is requested to perform contrast injection via nephrostomy to evaluate tube position/patency and degree of ureteral obstruction to guide next management, considering comorbid atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation (I48.91) and hypertension (I10). Specialist evaluation is referenced in care planning to coordinate findings and subsequent urinary tract management.

Objective evidence: 04-18-2026: Creatinine 1.01 mg/dL, eGFR 61 mL/min/1.73m², BUN 17 mg/dL, Potassium 4.0 mmol/L, Hgb 12.5 g/dL | 04-10-2026: Creatinine 1.02 (eGFR, eGFR 60 ), BUN 18, Potassium 4.2, Hgb 12.8 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: N13.2 - Hydronephrosis with renal and ureteral calculous obstruction
Z43.6 - Encounter for attention to cystostomy
pa_request.previous_treatments: Tamsulosin nightly and symptomatic management with acetaminophen and ondansetron; completed Augmentin course previously for urinary symptoms in nephrostomy tube care and a short course of phenazopyridine for dysuria; chronic hypertension control with lisinopril and anticoagulation with apixaban with planned peri-procedure hold.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Define collecting system/ureteral patency and obstruction level via nephrostomy injection to support informed planning for next steps in nephrostomy management and urinary obstruction treatment.

116.bio_narrative: Elliot Reyes is a 70-year-old Hispanic woman living in Dallas, Texas, who maintains a structured daily routine and tracks her medications carefully using a pill organizer. She works in an administrative role with long workdays that often lead to frequent takeout meals. She reports brisk treadmill walking about 5 days per week and values clear, step-by-step explanations during medical visits. She has a history of health-related anxiety and has been engaged in weekly cognitive-behavioral therapy, which she describes as helpful for managing worry and reassurance-seeking around procedures. In early February 2026, Elliot developed worsening right-sided flank and abdominal discomfort with urinary symptoms, and evaluation led to management for hydronephrosis with renal and ureteral calculous obstruction (N13.2). A right percutaneous nephrostomy tube was placed on 02-09-2026 to relieve obstruction and support urinary drainage. Since placement, she has continued to experience intermittent aching and cramping pain episodes (R10.9) and has noticed variable nephrostomy tube output, prompting repeat assessments for tube function and urinary tract patency. She has used tamsulosin nightly to support stone/obstruction symptom management and relies on acetaminophen for pain and ondansetron ODT for nausea during symptom flares. She previously completed a 7-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for urinary tract infection symptoms in the context of nephrostomy tube care (Z43.6), as well as a short course of phenazopyridine for transient urinary discomfort. Her medical history includes essential (primary) hypertension (I10) treated with lisinopril and atrial fibrillation (I48.91) managed with apixaban for stroke risk reduction, which adds complexity to peri-procedural planning due to bleeding considerations. Family history is notable for hypertension in both parents and kidney stones in an older brother. Over March and April 2026, she documented her tube output and symptoms and sought reassessment due to persistent discomfort. Objective testing included blood work for baseline CBC and metabolic status and imaging (renal ultrasound and CT) to characterize ongoing hydronephrosis, stone location, and nephrostomy position. Given her ongoing symptoms, indwelling nephrostomy status (Z43.6), and need to clarify collecting system and ureteral patency, she is scheduled for an injection procedure for nephrostogram and/or ureterogram (CPT 50394). The goal is to define the level of obstruction and assess tube position/function so her care team can plan next steps while accounting for anticoagulation and blood pressure management.

encounters.facility: Baylor Scott & White Primary Care - Uptown Dallas
encounters.chief_complaint: My nephrostomy tube output seems inconsistent and my right side aches on and off.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 70-year-old female with right percutaneous nephrostomy placed 02-09-2026 for hydronephrosis with renal and ureteral calculous obstruction (N13.2). She reports intermittent right flank/abdominal pain and cramping with occasional nausea. Nephrostomy tube output varies day to day; she has been measuring output and notes occasional decrease without complete cessation. Denies fever or chills today. She is concerned about tube position and whether obstruction is improving.

Review of Systems: Positive for intermittent flank/abdominal pain and nausea. Negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, dysuria currently, or gross hematuria. No new leg swelling. 

Physical Exam: Afebrile, in no acute distress. Abdomen soft, mild right CVA tenderness. Nephrostomy site clean/dry with intact dressing; no surrounding erythema, warmth, or purulent drainage. Cardiovascular irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs appreciated today. Lungs clear to auscultation.

Assessment: (1) N13.2 hydronephrosis with obstructing calculus, status post nephrostomy; ongoing intermittent pain and variable output. (2) Z43.6 attention to nephrostomy tube. (3) R10.9 intermittent abdominal/flank pain. (4) I10 hypertension, above goal today. (5) I48.91 atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation.

Plan: Continue tamsulosin nightly. Use acetaminophen for pain and ondansetron ODT PRN nausea. Reviewed nephrostomy care and red flags. Continue lisinopril; monitor BP at home and bring readings. Discussed anticoagulation management considerations for upcoming nephrostogram/ureterogram injection (CPT 50394) and will coordinate timing closer to procedure. Order basic labs for baseline and plan imaging prior to the requested procedure to assess collecting system status.

Mild right CVA tenderness

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient has ESRD on maintenance hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) due to hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0) with documented complications including recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5), anemia in CKD (D63.1), and secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81) despite dialysis and directed medical therapy. Transplant committee review and preoperative evaluations (labs, CT, cardiac clearance) have been completed to support proceeding with requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360).

Objective evidence: 04-12-2026: CO2 35 mEq/L., Potassium 5.2 -5.8 | 04-02-2026: Creatinine 9.8 mg/dL, BUN 62 mg/dL, CO2 19 mmol/L, Potassium 5.3 mmol/L, Hgb 9.6 g/dL.

pa_request.expected_outcome: Transition from dialysis dependence with improved metabolic stability related to ESRD and reduction in dialysis-related complication burden while maintaining close post-transplant monitoring.

115.bio_narrative: Mateo Sosa is a 63-year-old Hispanic male living in Dallas, Texas, who has been managing progressive kidney disease that culminated in end-stage renal disease (N18.6) and dependence on in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2). He works in a light-duty supervisory role and structures his week around a thrice-weekly dialysis schedule. Over the past year, he reports that dialysis has increasingly limited his energy and ability to sustain activity on non-dialysis days. At his 02-20-2026 specialist consult he described feeling “worn down” by dialysis, with fatigue after treatments and reduced exercise tolerance, prompting him to pursue transplant evaluation. His ESRD is attributed to hypertensive chronic kidney disease with ESRD (I12.0). Despite adherence to dialysis and medications, he has ongoing ESRD-related metabolic complications documented in his transplant workup: recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5) requiring potassium binder therapy on non-dialysis days, anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) managed with epoetin alfa administered during dialysis, and secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81) with hyperphosphatemia requiring sevelamer carbonate and calcitriol. Prior potassium binder therapy with patiromer was used in the past and discontinued due to constipation, and his regimen was adjusted to sodium zirconium cyclosilicate. Access history includes a left arm AV fistula created on 05-28-2024 and a tunneled hemodialysis catheter inserted on 06-14-2024 as bridge access; dialysis documentation from March–April 2026 demonstrates continued dialysis dependence and ongoing electrolyte management needs. As part of transplant preparation, he completed a transplant surgery consult on 03-25-2026 to review the operative approach for renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) and to outline next steps. Pre-transplant labs on 04-02-2026 showed a pattern consistent with ESRD and CKD anemia, with potassium elevation consistent with his hyperkalemia history. CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast on 04-09-2026 characterized atrophic native kidneys and iliac vessel anatomy for operative planning. On 04-03-2026, cardiac clearance evaluation included ECG and echocardiography to assess perioperative risk in the context of long-standing hypertension. Mateo reports no current tobacco use and drinks alcohol socially a few times per month. He denies illicit drug use. He uses a pill organizer and reminders and is generally adherent with treatments; he notes stress-related irritability when schedules change. He has mild situational anxiety related to the transplant process and finances (F41.9) and remains engaged in monthly medication management visits. Family history is notable for hypertension in first-degree relatives, which he associates with his long-standing blood pressure issues. With transplant committee approval documented on 04-15-2026 and a planned date of 05-26-2026, the current request is to proceed with renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) to address dialysis dependence and persistent ESRD complication burden.

114.bio_narrative: Caroline Channing is a 64-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas who has been maintained on long-term in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2) for end-stage renal disease (N18.6) attributed to longstanding hypertension (I10) with hypertensive chronic kidney disease/ESRD (I12.0). She works part-time in customer service and relies on a tight weekly schedule to coordinate transportation and dialysis appointments. She uses a pill organizer and handwritten checklists to manage medications and dialysis-related tasks; she reports frustration with the complexity of her regimen but follows through when plans are clear. She does not use tobacco, drinks alcohol rarely, and denies illicit drug use. Over the past two years, Caroline has required ongoing medical management of CKD complications, including anemia of chronic kidney disease (D63.1) treated with epoetin alfa and IV iron during dialysis, and mineral-bone disorder/secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81) treated with sevelamer carbonate and calcitriol. Despite adherence to thrice-weekly treatments and careful medication adjustments, she continues to experience significant dialysis-related fatigue, intermittent nausea (managed with ondansetron), and variable interdialytic weight gain that she associates with workday reliance on convenience foods. She reports anxiety and irritability surrounding transplant uncertainty and finances and has remained engaged in weekly cognitive-behavioral therapy. In early March 2026, she presented with dysuria and suprapubic pressure and was treated for urinary tract infection (N39.0). During subsequent transplant evaluation visits, recurrent UTI history and intermittent native kidney discomfort (N28.89) were discussed as factors that could complicate perioperative management, prompting coordination with infectious disease and surgical teams. Her pre-transplant workup included a transthoracic echocardiogram on 05-08-2026 to assess cardiac function and perioperative risk in the context of hypertension, a CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast on 05-15-2026 to evaluate pelvic vasculature and native kidney anatomy for operative planning, and a duplex ultrasound of her left AV fistula on 05-16-2026 to confirm continued patency for dialysis access up to the scheduled surgery. Pre-op labs on 05-18-2026 documented anemia indices, electrolyte control, coagulation parameters, and mineral-bone markers needed for dialysis and perioperative planning. Family history is notable for hypertension in her father and type 2 diabetes in her mother; she reports no known hereditary kidney disease. She has a reliable support person who can assist with post-operative appointments and medication organization. Given her dialysis dependence with persistent symptom burden, CKD complications, and the native kidney infection/symptom history, she is proceeding with a requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) scheduled for 06-10-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of dysuria and suprapubic pressure with concern for urinary tract infection in a dialysis-dependent ESRD patient.
encounters.chief_complaint: Burning when I try to pee and lower belly pressure.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient with ESRD on hemodialysis (Z99.2) reports 3 days of burning with urination and suprapubic pressure. She notes decreased urine output baseline due to dialysis but still voids small amounts. Denies flank trauma; endorses mild nausea. No fevers or rigors. Reports prior UTIs in past year.

Review of Systems: Positive for dysuria, suprapubic discomfort, urinary urgency with scant output, and mild nausea. Negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, vomiting, or new edema beyond baseline interdialytic changes.

Physical Exam: Afebrile, no acute distress. Abdomen soft, mild suprapubic tenderness without rebound/guarding. No CVA tenderness. Cardiovascular regular rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs clear.

Assessment: Suspected urinary tract infection (N39.0) in patient with ESRD (N18.6) on dialysis (Z99.2) and hypertensive CKD/ESRD (I12.0). Hypertension (I10) above goal today.

Plan: Obtain urinalysis and urine culture if adequate specimen; start cephalexin course given symptoms and prior response; counsel on monitoring for fever/chills and ER precautions. Continue amlodipine and carvedilol; reinforce low-sodium diet and dialysis fluid restriction. Notify nephrology team of infection treatment given transplant evaluation trajectory.

encounters.chief_complaint: I want to be sure infections won’t delay my transplant.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) referred for pre-transplant infectious disease review. She had symptomatic UTI in March (N39.0) treated with cephalexin with symptom resolution. She reports intermittent dysuria episodes historically with limited urine output and occasional lower abdominal pressure. She also reports intermittent native kidney discomfort (N28.89). Penicillin allergy described as hives and facial itching; shellfish allergy with lip swelling/throat itching.

Review of Systems: Negative for fever, chills, night sweats, cough, or skin infection. Positive for intermittent urinary symptoms historically; currently asymptomatic.

Physical Exam: Afebrile, well-appearing. No suprapubic tenderness today. AV fistula site clean and intact.

Assessment: Recurrent UTI history (N39.0) in dialysis-dependent ESRD (N18.6, Z99.2) undergoing workup for renal transplant with planned recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360 requested). Allergies may influence perioperative antimicrobial selection.

Plan: Recommend obtaining perioperative urine culture if patient is producing urine and review prior culture susceptibilities; coordinate with transplant team for antimicrobial prophylaxis strategy that avoids penicillins. Continue Bactrim DS three times weekly per nephrology/transplant protocol planning. Counsel on early reporting of urinary symptoms before surgery and avoidance of unnecessary antibiotics. Vaccination history reviewed and documented as up to date for influenza, pneumococcal, hepatitis B, COVID-19, Tdap, and shingles.
observations: Currently asymptomatic for infection; no systemic signs
Drug allergy history reviewed for perioperative planning
Education provided on symptom monitoring and culture collection

encounters.progress_notes: Infection risk factors reviewed; patient understands plan to report symptoms early and to avoid self-starting antibiotics. Coordination message sent to transplant surgery and nephrology regarding allergy-aware perioperative prophylaxis planning.
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Return to ID clinic if urinary symptoms recur prior to surgery or if advised by transplant team. Maintain dialysis schedule and continue prophylaxis per protocol.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Pre-operative evaluation and medication planning for scheduled renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy.
encounters.chief_complaint: I’m here to get cleared and understand what to do with my meds before surgery.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 64-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) due to hypertensive CKD/ESRD (I12.0) presents for pre-op evaluation for renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360 requested), anticipated 06-10-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital. She reports stable dialysis attendance and no current infectious symptoms. She continues antihypertensive therapy (amlodipine, carvedilol). CKD complications include anemia (D63.1) managed with epoetin alfa and IV iron, and secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81) treated with sevelamer and calcitriol. She notes intermittent nausea but controlled with ondansetron.

Review of Systems: Negative for fever/chills, chest pain, dyspnea at rest, or active dysuria. Positive for chronic fatigue and intermittent nausea.

Physical Exam: Alert and oriented, no distress. Cardiovascular: regular rhythm; no edema. Respiratory: clear. Abdomen: soft, non-tender. Vascular access: left AV fistula with strong thrill, no erythema.

Assessment: Pre-op evaluation for requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360). ESRD on dialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) with hypertension (I10, I12.0), anemia in CKD (D63.1), secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81), and history of UTI (N39.0) and symptomatic native kidney disorder (N28.89).

Plan: Reviewed pre-op labs from 05-18-2026 and diagnostic workup (echo 05-08-2026, CT 05-15-2026, AVF duplex 05-16-2026) supporting operative planning. Medication plan: continue carvedilol and amlodipine morning of surgery unless anesthesia advises otherwise; continue sevelamer and calcitriol until NPO instructions begin; dialysis unit to coordinate final pre-op dialysis within 24 hours of surgery date per transplant protocol. Reinforced avoidance of NSAIDs; ibuprofen already held as of 05-26-2026 per plan. Infection precautions reviewed; continue Bactrim DS as directed unless transplant team instructs hold on day of surgery. Concurrent Care Reference: patient is in active weekly Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy with Dr. Monica Geller, PhD; discussed maintaining therapy around perioperative stress.
observations: Pre-op workup reviewed and documented for transplant planning
AV fistula patent by exam; no signs of access infection
Medication reconciliation performed with explicit NSAID avoidance

images.findings: Obtained as part of pre-operative evaluation for requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360). Left ventricular ejection fraction estimated 60-65% with normal chamber sizes and no regional wall motion abnormalities. Mild concentric LV hypertrophy consistent with long-standing hypertension (I10/I12.0). No more than mild valvular regurgitation; no pericardial effusion.

Performed for operative planning prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360). Native kidneys are small and atrophic with cortical thinning; no hydronephrosis or obstructing stone. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of iliac vessels without severe stenosis by non-contrast assessment; anatomy appears suitable for transplant anastomosis planning. No intra-abdominal abscess or acute inflammatory process identified.

Ordered to support perioperative dialysis planning ahead of requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360). Left radiocephalic AV fistula is patent with continuous thrill; no evidence of acute thrombosis. Flow volumes are adequate for ongoing hemodialysis; no hemodynamically significant stenosis identified in the evaluated segments.

Mineral-bone: Phosphorus 6.1 mg/dL, corrected calcium 8.6 mg/dL, intact PTH 612 pg/mL (findings consistent with N25.81).

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient has end stage renal disease (N18.6) with hypertensive CKD with ESRD (I12.0) and ongoing uremic symptoms and volume overload (R60.0) with metabolic complications (E87.2) and anemia of CKD (D63.1). Pre-dialysis workup and planning have been completed (renal labs, chest X-ray, ECG, and vascular mapping), and dialysis services are requested to support solute and volume management while care coordination establishes longitudinal dialysis oversight.

Objective evidence: 04-14-2026: Creatinine 7.4 mg/dL, BUN 86 mg/dL, CO2 16 mmol/L, Potassium 5.5 mmol/L, Hgb 9.0 g/dL | 04-02-2026: Creatinine 6.9 mg/dL, BUN 78 mg/dL, Potassium 5.3 mmol/L, Hgb 9.2 g/dL.

D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Amlodipine, furosemide, sodium bicarbonate, ferrous sulfate; past ondansetron course for nausea; dietary sodium reduction efforts; psychotherapy support.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved management of uremic symptoms and fluid status with safer control of metabolic complications in ESRD while completing dialysis start coordination.

110.bio_narrative: Zoey Buffay is a 63-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who has been followed by her primary care physician, Dr. Carlos Mendez, MD, for long-standing hypertension complicated by progressive chronic kidney disease that has advanced to end stage renal disease (N18.6) with hypertensive chronic kidney disease with ESRD (I12.0). Over the past year she has noticed a gradual decline in stamina, and in the months leading up to April 2026 she reported persistent fatigue that limits routine activities, intermittent nausea with reduced appetite, and recurrent swelling of her ankles and lower legs (R60.0). She has tried to manage symptoms with careful attention to sodium intake, timing medications with food when able, and close follow-up visits. On 11-18-2025 she presented to the emergency department for inability to keep food down with worsening leg swelling, which was treated supportively and reinforced the need for close renal monitoring. In late March 2026 she returned to clinic with worsening exhaustion and edema; laboratory monitoring was pursued and she continued supportive medical therapy including amlodipine for blood pressure control, furosemide for symptom-directed volume management, sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis (E87.2), and ferrous sulfate for anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1). A short prior course of ondansetron was used for nausea, and she uses acetaminophen for aches while avoiding NSAIDs in ESRD. In early April 2026, pre-dialysis planning progressed with baseline studies obtained as part of preparation for dialysis: a chest X-ray (CPT 71046 on 04-03-2026) and vascular access mapping ultrasound (CPT 93990 on 04-08-2026). On 04-15-2026 she underwent a pre-op evaluation with a 12-lead ECG (CPT 93000) and a medication review. At her follow-up on 04-16-2026, she described continued fatigue and intermittent edema and reported she was actively trying to get dialysis set up but did not yet know which nephrologist would be overseeing her dialysis plan. Zoey lives alone but has family support in Houston and relies on reminders to keep appointments. She is a former light smoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and denies illicit drug use. She is engaged in ongoing psychotherapy for situational anxiety related to medical visits and the dialysis transition, working on coping strategies and adherence routines. Family history is notable for hypertension in first-degree relatives and a parent with type 2 diabetes, contributing to her risk profile for kidney disease progression. Current planning focuses on initiating dialysis services (CPT 90999) with coordination of longitudinal nephrology oversight and ongoing monitoring of volume status and metabolic complications.

110.behavioral_notes: Zoey reports she generally takes daily medications as directed but has a tendency to delay doses when she feels nauseated or overwhelmed by appointments. She is a former light smoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and denies illicit drug use. She describes situational anxiety around medical visits and the dialysis transition and is engaged in psychotherapy to improve coping and adherence routines.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of nausea/vomiting with inability to keep food down and worsening lower-extremity swelling in the setting of known advanced kidney disease.
encounters.chief_complaint: I can’t keep food down and my legs are swelling up.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 63-year-old female with known advanced CKD presents with 3 days of nausea and poor PO intake with two episodes of non-bloody emesis and progressive bilateral ankle/leg swelling. Reports increasing fatigue over weeks and decreased urine output compared with baseline. Denies chest pain, fevers, or focal abdominal pain. 

Review of Systems: Positive for nausea, decreased appetite, fatigue, and leg swelling. Negative for shortness of breath at rest, cough, dysuria, rash, or syncope.

Physical Exam: Appears tired but non-toxic. Lungs clear to auscultation without rales. Cardiovascular regular rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no rebound. Extremities with 2+ pitting edema to mid-shins bilaterally; no calf tenderness. Neuro intact.

Assessment: Uremic symptoms with volume overload in advanced kidney disease; concern for progression to ESRD (N18.6). 

Plan: Symptom control with antiemetic; counsel on low sodium diet, fluid balance awareness, and close follow-up with PCP for renal lab monitoring. Avoid NSAIDs; continue BP management plan. Return precautions for dyspnea, chest pain, confusion, or inability to tolerate PO.
observations: Bilateral 2+ pitting edema to mid-shins

No rales on lung exam

encounters.follow_up_instructions: Follow up with Dr. Carlos Mendez within 3-5 days for kidney function and electrolytes. Continue low sodium diet. Return to ER for worsening shortness of breath, confusion, chest pain, or inability to keep fluids down.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: Patient with hypertensive CKD now documented as ESRD reports 2-3 months of progressive fatigue with intermittent nausea and recurrent ankle swelling, worse at end of day. Reports adherence to amlodipine; takes diuretic most days but sometimes delays when nausea is prominent. No chest pain; occasional mild dyspnea on exertion when climbing stairs.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance, edema, intermittent nausea. Negative for fever, productive cough, dysuria, or unilateral leg pain.

Physical Exam: BP elevated. Cardiopulmonary exam without crackles or wheeze; regular rhythm. Abdomen benign. 1-2+ pitting edema at ankles bilaterally.

Assessment: ESRD (N18.6) in setting of hypertensive CKD with ESRD (I12.0) with volume symptoms (R60.0) and suspected metabolic acidosis (E87.2). 

Plan: Order BMP/CBC/iron indices to assess metabolic status and anemia. Start sodium bicarbonate for acidosis management pending labs. Reinforce sodium restriction and daily weights. Discuss dialysis planning and need for access evaluation; refer for vascular mapping. Continue current BP regimen; counsel to avoid NSAIDs.

221.bio_narrative: Din Djarin is a 72-year-old male living in downtown Houston, Texas, who works nights as a building security supervisor. Over the past several years he has managed type 2 diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease (E11.22) and hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0), with progressive decline in kidney function to chronic kidney disease stage 5 (N18.5) and now end stage renal disease (N18.6). He is routine-oriented and relies on a written medication schedule and pill organizer, but his night-shift routine and reliance on prepared foods have made dietary potassium and phosphorus restriction challenging. In late 2025 he underwent AV fistula creation (CPT 36821) as renal function worsened. Despite intensified outpatient management, he developed increasing fatigue and worsening ankle swelling (R60.0) noted at his 02-24-2026 primary care visit. By March 2026, he reported daily nausea, reduced appetite, and diffuse pruritus consistent with uremic symptom burden, and nephrology documented ongoing metabolic complications. A retroperitoneal ultrasound on 04-02-2026 supported chronic intrinsic renal disease without hydronephrosis, and on 04-03-2026 he presented to the emergency department after outpatient labs showed recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5), requiring urgent stabilization and reinforcing the need for renal replacement therapy planning. He has been treated with furosemide for persistent volume overload (R60.0), sodium zirconium cyclosilicate for recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5), sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis (E87.2), and calcium acetate plus calcitriol for mineral bone disorder and secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81). He also has anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1); epoetin alfa-epbx was previously trialed and is now on hold pending transition to dialysis unit protocol. A tunneled hemodialysis catheter was placed (CPT 36558) to ensure access while his fistula matures. With persistent symptoms and recurrent metabolic derangements despite medical therapy, nephrology is requesting initiation of dialysis (CPT 90999) with a planned outpatient start date. Family history is notable for diabetes and hypertension in first-degree relatives, with a brother who developed kidney disease later in life. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and denies illicit drug use. He completed a short course of supportive psychotherapy for stress management related to chronic illness and reports using breathing and relaxation techniques to maintain appointment follow-through as dialysis planning proceeds.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of worsening fatigue and ankle swelling in the setting of advanced CKD with multiple comorbidities.
encounters.chief_complaint: I’m exhausted all the time and my ankles keep swelling.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 72-year-old male with CKD stage 5 (N18.5) with progression toward ESRD (N18.6), hypertensive CKD (I12.0), and diabetes with CKD (E11.22) presents with 6–8 weeks of worsening fatigue and bilateral ankle swelling. Reports tighter shoes by end of shift and mild shortness of breath when climbing stairs. Denies chest pain. Admits difficulty keeping potassium and phosphorus low due to night-shift meals.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and lower-extremity swelling. Negative for fever, cough, chest pain, syncope. GI: intermittent nausea. Skin: intermittent itching. GU: decreased urine volume compared to prior months.

Physical Exam: BP elevated. Lungs with faint bibasilar crackles. Cardiovascular regular rhythm, no murmurs. 2+ pitting edema to mid-shin bilaterally. No focal neurologic deficits.

Assessment: Volume overload/edema (R60.0) in advanced CKD (N18.5) approaching ESRD (N18.6). Hypertensive CKD (I12.0). Type 2 diabetes with CKD (E11.22).

Plan: Increase loop diuretic (furosemide) and reinforce low-sodium diet; review home BP log. Coordinate nephrology follow-up for dialysis planning. Counsel on low potassium/phosphorus diet and provide renal dietitian referral. Order labs through nephrology; advise ER precautions for worsening dyspnea, chest pain, confusion, or severe weakness.

Mild exertional dyspnea
Dietary potassium/phosphorus challenges due to night shift

Reports intermittent palpitations and muscle weakness episodes; prior labs have shown elevated potassium.

Physical Exam: Chronically ill-appearing, no acute distress. 2+ edema lower extremities. Skin excoriations on forearms from scratching. Lungs clear to auscultation with mild basilar diminution. 

Assessment: ESRD (N18.6) with recurrent hyperkalemia risk (E87.5), metabolic acidosis (E87.2), edema/volume overload (R60.0), anemia of CKD (D63.1) by prior trends, and secondary hyperparathyroidism/mineral bone disorder (N25.81). Contributing comorbidities include I12.0 and E11.22.

Plan: Continue dialysis planning; review access history (AV fistula created 12-18-2025; tunneled catheter already placed 01-05-2026). Start/continue Lokelma on non-dialysis days until dialysis initiation; start sodium bicarbonate for acidosis; start calcium acetate with meals and calcitriol for mineral bone disorder; review renal diet (potassium/phosphorus). Order renal ultrasound to exclude obstruction and obtain updated labs (BMP/CMP, phosphorus, albumin, CBC, iron studies, PTH, vitamin D). Discuss symptoms as consistent with uremia and plan for in-center hemodialysis start once logistics finalized.

images.findings: Ultrasound demonstrates bilateral kidneys with increased cortical echogenicity and mild cortical thinning, consistent with chronic medical renal disease. No hydronephrosis or obstructing calculus is visualized. Bladder is partially distended without focal mass. Findings support intrinsic chronic kidney disease without evidence of reversible obstructive uropathy in the workup preceding planned dialysis initiation (CPT 90999).

reports.results: Basic/Chemistry panel notable for advanced renal dysfunction pattern with impaired clearance and recurrent electrolyte disturbance history in ESRD (N18.6). Potassium trend remains labile consistent with recurrent hyperkalemia risk (E87.5) despite binder therapy; bicarbonate/CO2 is reduced consistent with metabolic acidosis (E87.2). Phosphorus is elevated consistent with renal mineral bone disorder/secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81). Albumin shows low-normal to mildly low trend consistent with reduced nutritional intake related to uremic nausea.
reports.notes: Labs obtained as part of pre-dialysis planning and to guide initiation of dialysis (CPT 90999) including assessment of electrolyte/acid-base status, phosphorus burden, and nutritional markers. Values reviewed with patient during pre-op evaluation and follow-up; continued interim medications (Lokelma, sodium bicarbonate, calcium acetate) pending dialysis start.

reports.results: CBC demonstrates anemia pattern consistent with anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1). Iron studies used to assess iron availability prior to dialysis-unit anemia protocol transition; no evidence of acute blood loss by history. PTH is elevated consistent with secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81); vitamin D level used to guide calcitriol therapy.
reports.notes: Obtained in pre-dialysis workup to quantify anemia severity and mineral bone disorder parameters prior to starting dialysis (CPT 90999). Prior epoetin alfa-epbx trial is documented as past therapy and currently on hold to transition management to dialysis unit protocol.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with end-stage renal disease (N18.6) has progressive uremic symptoms (fatigue, pruritus) and recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5) with metabolic acidosis (E87.21) despite ongoing medical therapy including patiromer and sodium bicarbonate. Comorbid hypertensive CKD (I12.0) and chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) with edema (R60.0) complicate volume management; initiation of outpatient hemodialysis is requested for solute and fluid control.

Objective evidence: 04-10-2026: Creatinine 6.8 mg/dL, BUN 84 mg/dL, Potassium 5.6 mmol/L, Hgb 9.4 g/dL | 03-29-2026: Creatinine 6.4 mg/dL, BUN 78 mg/dL, Potassium 5.8 mmol/L, Hgb 9.6 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Medical management with loop diuretic for volume control; potassium binder therapy (patiromer) and renal diet for recurrent hyperkalemia; sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis; prior ACE-inhibitor (lisinopril) stopped due to hyperkalemia as renal function declined; ongoing BP control with amlodipine and carvedilol; anemia support with oral iron.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved fluid and solute management (potassium and acid-base control), decreased symptom burden (fatigue, pruritus), and improved functional tolerance while continuing comorbidity management.

111.bio_narrative: Cassian Korr is a 71-year-old Hispanic male living in Houston, Texas with a long history of hypertension complicated by hypertensive chronic kidney disease that has progressed to end-stage renal disease (I12.0, N18.6). Over the last year he has noticed a steady decline in stamina and increasing difficulty managing fluid balance at home. In early 2026 his symptoms intensified: he described marked fatigue, worsening bilateral ankle swelling, and reduced exertional capacity with more frequent shortness of breath on routine activities, consistent with challenges of volume control in the setting of chronic diastolic heart failure and ESRD (I50.32, R60.0, N18.6). He also reports persistent generalized itching that has become disruptive to sleep and daily functioning (L29.9). He remains generally adherent with prescribed medications and brings written logs of blood pressure and daily weights to clinic visits. His current regimen includes furosemide for edema and heart failure, carvedilol and amlodipine for blood pressure and heart rate control, atorvastatin for cardiovascular risk management, and sertraline for adjustment disorder with anxiety (F43.22) related to chronic illness. As renal function declined, lisinopril was discontinued due to worsening hyperkalemia (E87.5). He is followed by nephrology for management of ESRD complications including recurrent hyperkalemia treated with patiromer (E87.5) and metabolic acidosis treated with sodium bicarbonate (E87.21). He has anemia in chronic kidney disease treated with oral iron support (D63.1). In February 2026 he presented to primary care reporting exhaustion and increased leg swelling, prompting closer monitoring and nephrology coordination. On 03-15-2026 he went to the emergency department for palpitations and muscle weakness, where he underwent potassium management and monitoring for hyperkalemia risk in ESRD. Subsequent outpatient labs (BMP/CBC) and nephrology evaluations documented ongoing metabolic abnormalities and symptom burden despite medical therapy. A nephrology specialist consultation on 04-03-2026 initiated dialysis education and planning, and a vascular access evaluation was completed on 04-08-2026. At his 04-14-2026 pre-procedure evaluation he continued to report severe fatigue, pruritus, and dyspnea on exertion, supporting a plan to initiate outpatient hemodialysis (CPT 90999) with an expected start date of 05-18-2026. Socially, he is married and lives with his spouse who helps with transportation and appointment scheduling. He is a former smoker and drinks alcohol rarely (about 1–2 drinks per month), with no illicit drug use. He follows a renal diet with occasional high-sodium lapses that correlate with increased swelling, and he is actively working on meal planning and label reading. Family history is notable for hypertension in multiple first-degree relatives and a father with chronic kidney disease later in life. The current referral for dialysis initiation is based on progressive ESRD symptoms (fatigue, pruritus), recurrent electrolyte/acid-base complications (E87.5, E87.21), and ongoing volume management challenges (R60.0) in the context of hypertensive CKD and heart failure (I12.0, I50.32).

2231.procedure_requested: Insertion of tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with ESRD on chronic hemodialysis has recurrent dialysis catheter dysfunction leading to shortened or incomplete treatments and documented electrolyte instability. A tunneled dialysis catheter is requested to provide reliable long-term access while his AV fistula matures, reducing repeated emergent non-tunneled line placements. Comorbid hypertension and diabetes increase clinical risk during missed dialysis episodes and support need for stable access.

Objective evidence: 04-10-2026: Potassium 5.9 mmol/L | 04-09-2026: Creatinine 9.8 mg/dL, BUN 74 mg/dL, CO2 20 mmol/L, Potassium 5.4 mmol/L, Hgb 10.2 g/dL.

pa_request.previous_treatments: Dialysis unit catheter troubleshooting, heparin lock protocols, prior non-tunneled catheter placement (CPT 36556), and AV fistula creation with ongoing maturation monitoring (CPT 36821).
pa_request.expected_outcome: More reliable hemodialysis access enabling completion of prescribed dialysis sessions with stable blood flow rates to support ongoing fluid and electrolyte management.

2231.bio_narrative: Leonard Hofstadter is a 71-year-old man living in Houston, Texas, with a long-standing pattern of meticulous self-monitoring and a tendency toward anxious rumination when medical plans change. Over the last several years he has managed progressive kidney disease that ultimately advanced to end-stage renal disease (N18.6). He started in-center hemodialysis in late 2024 and remains dialysis-dependent (Z99.2). He keeps a detailed calendar around his Monday/Wednesday/Friday dialysis schedule, but repeated access setbacks have disrupted his routine and increased his worry about falling behind on treatments. In early 2026 he experienced increasing problems with dialysis access function, with dialysis staff documenting frequent pressure alarms and inability to sustain ordered blood flow rates, consistent with a complication of his dialysis catheter (T82.49XA). These access problems led to shortened treatments and worsening post-dialysis fatigue. In April 2026 he presented to the emergency department after a session ended early; laboratory testing showed hyperkalemia (E87.5) and uremia consistent with ESRD physiology. He also carries hypertensive chronic kidney disease with ESRD (I12.0) and type 2 diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease (E11.22), both of which increase vulnerability to rapid fluid and electrolyte shifts when dialysis runs are incomplete. His dialysis team and nephrologist have pursued steps toward durable access, including creation of a left forearm arteriovenous fistula (CPT 36821, completed 03-05-2026). The fistula currently has a palpable thrill and bruit but has not been reliably usable for full treatments, and adequacy monitoring visits have continued (Z49.31). Given recurrent catheter dysfunction and the need to ensure reliable ongoing dialysis while the fistula matures, vascular surgery evaluated him on 04-15-2026 and recommended insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558, requested). The goal is to provide stable long-term access to support consistent delivery of prescribed dialysis sessions and reduce repeated emergent placement of temporary lines. He is married and relies on his spouse for transportation on procedure days and for reminders about dressing care. He is a former light smoker, does not use illicit drugs, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is notable for diabetes and hypertension, aligning with his current comorbidities and long-term renal disease trajectory.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient with confirmed intrauterine pregnancy at 7 weeks gestation is requesting outpatient medication abortion using mifepristone. She has persistent nausea/vomiting and intermittent pelvic cramping affecting daily function despite supportive antiemetics, and desires a non-surgical outpatient option. Migraine history is noted for pain-control planning during the medication course.

Objective evidence: 04-12-2026: Hgb 12.8 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: G43.009 - Migraine without aura, not intractable, without status migrainosus
pa_request.previous_treatments: Diclegis nightly with partial relief; ondansetron ODT PRN with variable relief; acetaminophen PRN for pain; prior propranolol ER for migraine prophylaxis stopped due to fatigue.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Completion of outpatient medication abortion course with follow-up confirmation and symptom management.

268.bio_narrative: Pamela Beesly is a 27-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas who is followed by primary care at UT Southwestern Medical Center. She maintains a structured routine, exercises vigorously most weekdays, and keeps a written log of symptoms and medication doses. She reports rare alcohol use and denies tobacco or illicit drug use. She has a history of migraine without aura (G43.009), previously trialing propranolol ER for prevention but stopping it in late 2024 due to fatigue; she currently uses sumatriptan at onset and acetaminophen as needed. In late March 2026, Pamela completed two home pregnancy tests that were positive and scheduled a telehealth visit on 03-27-2026 due to daily nausea and vomiting. Supportive management for nausea in early pregnancy (R11.2) was started with doxylamine-pyridoxine and ondansetron ODT for breakthrough symptoms, along with constipation prevention using docusate. She also noted intermittent pelvic cramping (R10.2) without heavy bleeding, which was managed symptomatically with acetaminophen and a plan for careful use of NSAIDs per counseling. On 04-11-2026 she attended a specialist consultation for pregnancy dating and options counseling. A transvaginal pelvic ultrasound confirmed a single live intrauterine pregnancy consistent with 7 weeks gestation (Z3A.07) and pregnancy state (Z33.1), aligning with an outpatient medication window. Baseline laboratory testing on 04-12-2026 (CBC, ABO/Rh, urinalysis, GC/CT NAAT) was obtained as part of pre-treatment assessment and to support planning for the requested medication abortion using mifepristone. A pre-procedure evaluation on 04-18-2026 reviewed baseline labs and symptom-control planning, taking into account her migraine history and medication allergies (penicillin and latex). She returned on 04-24-2026 for an office visit due to a week of worsened migraines and discussed safe analgesic strategies while pregnant and in anticipation of the planned outpatient medication course. Family history is notable for maternal history of migraines and paternal history of hypertension. Pamela reports having stable housing, reliable transportation, and a supportive spouse who can assist with follow-up and monitoring during the medication course.

Plan: Start doxylamine-pyridoxine nightly; add ondansetron ODT PRN for breakthrough nausea; start docusate PRN constipation prevention. Counsel hydration and small frequent meals. Provide referral for pregnancy dating evaluation and discuss options counseling. Continue sumatriptan PRN per prior plan; prefer acetaminophen for pain. 269.bio_narrative: Pam Beesly is a 27-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas who is established with Dr. Maya R. Patel, MD at Austin Regional Primary Care. She has a history of migraine without aura (G43.009) that she typically manages with acetaminophen as needed, and she also uses allergy medications seasonally. She is generally organized and prefers written, step-by-step instructions, which has been incorporated into her care planning. She is engaged in weekly psychotherapy for stress management and reports mild anxiety symptoms that she manages with journaling and scheduling. In early April 2026, Pam reported a positive home pregnancy test and requested counseling on pregnancy options. During a telehealth visit on 04-03-2026, she discussed her symptoms and goals and indicated a preference for the medication option, pending confirmation of gestational age and pregnancy location. On 04-10-2026 she presented for an in-person visit for pregnancy confirmation and dating. A transvaginal pelvic ultrasound documented a single intrauterine pregnancy consistent with very early first trimester dating (Z3A.01) and did not show adnexal mass or significant free fluid. Baseline workup included CBC, ABO/Rh typing, and urine NAAT testing for gonorrhea and chlamydia. She also described pregnancy-related nausea (O26.891), for which ondansetron ODT was prescribed to help her tolerate the planned medication regimen. At follow-up on 04-18-2026, results were reviewed and the outpatient medication abortion plan was discussed. On 04-22-2026, a pre-procedure readiness evaluation reviewed REMS criteria elements and documented consent for medication abortion (Z33.2). Counseling included expected cramping and bleeding, warning signs requiring urgent evaluation, and a structured plan for check-ins and confirmation testing; the specific follow-up appointment time remains pending scheduling, and a coordination call on 04-24-2026 addressed logistics and reinforced return precautions. Pam has no illicit drug use, rarely drinks alcohol, and is a never-smoker. She walks several times per week. Family history is notable for a maternal history of migraine and a paternal history of hypertension; she reports strong support from her spouse. Her documented allergies to penicillin (hives/itching) and latex (localized rash) have been reviewed for clinic precautions and emergency planning.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient has myelodysplastic syndrome with symptomatic anemia and hemoglobin documented at 9.9 g/dL (05-07-2026) with fatigue and exertional dyspnea limiting activity. Epoetin alfa is requested due to persistent anemia and history of partial response to prior ESA therapy, with goal to improve hemoglobin trend and reduce transfusion exposure; coronary artery disease is a relevant comorbidity for careful anemia management. Supporting labs have been submitted; transferrin saturation and ferritin are pending review as part of iron availability assessment.

Objective evidence: 05-12-2026: Hgb 9.9 g/dL | 05-07-2026: Hgb 9.9 g/dL.
supporting_diagnoses: I25.10 - Atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary artery without angina pectoris
pa_request.previous_treatments: Prior ESA therapy trial in 2025 with partial response and improved fatigue; continued oral iron supplementation and updated anemia workup with ferritin/transferrin saturation pending review.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Moderate improvement in anemia-related symptoms and stabilization/upward trend in hemoglobin values with decreased need for transfusion support using serial CBC monitoring.

278.bio_narrative: Eldon Brandybuck is a 74-year-old man living in Austin, Texas with his spouse. He is a retired park maintenance supervisor who remains active around the house and enjoys early-morning walks, but over the past two months he has had a noticeable decline in stamina. He reports persistent fatigue and shortness of breath with routine exertion such as climbing a flight of stairs or carrying groceries, prompting re-evaluation of his chronic anemia. He describes himself as cautious and methodical with health decisions, preferring to understand the plan and to keep a steady routine; he tracks his medications and appointments in a pocket notebook and typically brings a family member to visits for support. His anemia history dates back to 2024 when routine labs began showing macrocytosis and a downward hemoglobin trend. A bone marrow biopsy performed on 03-22-2025 confirmed myelodysplastic syndrome with multilineage dysplasia (D46.9) and associated anemia (D64.9), without increased blasts. Following diagnosis, he trialed erythropoiesis-stimulating agent therapy in mid-2025 and experienced a partial response, with a modest improvement in hemoglobin and day-to-day energy per hematology follow-up notes; therapy was later paused during a period of relative stability. In early May 2026, his symptoms returned alongside a hemoglobin of 9.9 g/dL documented on 05-07-2026, and he again noted difficulty maintaining his normal activity level. He has a history of atherosclerotic heart disease without angina (I25.10), making avoidance of more severe anemia clinically important when planning ongoing care. His current workup has included CBC and iron study components; transferrin saturation and ferritin are pending review to assess iron availability and guide supplementation while using ESA therapy. With recurrent symptomatic anemia and prior partial response to ESA therapy, his hematologist has requested epoetin alfa injection (non-ESRD use) 1,000 units (HCPCS J0885) with close monitoring of blood pressure, symptoms, and serial hemoglobin trends. Family history is notable for a father with coronary artery disease and a mother who had anemia late in life of unclear cause. Eldon does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and stays engaged with his family and community, which he identifies as key to coping with chronic illness. The current referral focuses on safely supporting erythropoiesis and maintaining functional capacity while continuing diagnostic review of iron indices prior to the planned administration date.

277.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 73-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas, who presents with a several-month history of persistent fatigue (R53.83) and reduced exercise tolerance accompanied by intermittent exertional shortness of breath (R06.02). She describes a daily pattern of “heavy tiredness” that worsens in the afternoon and has led her to limit errands and household tasks. She reports occasional lightheadedness when standing quickly but denies chest pain. Her medical history is notable for essential hypertension (I10) managed with long-term antihypertensive therapy and obesity (E66.9), which she acknowledges has contributed to gradual deconditioning. Over the past 4–6 months, she has attempted conservative measures including hydration, sleep routine changes, and a graded walking plan; she also previously tried oral iron and a daily multivitamin during a period when she felt “run down,” but she did not perceive sustained improvement. Her social history includes living with her spouse and maintaining an independent routine with a preference for structured schedules. She reports a generally sedentary activity pattern with brief walks several times per week and periodic efforts to improve diet, though she often relies on convenience foods when fatigued. She reports alcohol use only occasionally and denies illicit drug use. She reports never using tobacco, though one specialty note references a remote smoking history in prior records. Family history is notable for cardiovascular risk factors and metabolic disease, which she views as a motivation to stay engaged with primary care and preventive screening. Clinically, her fatigue and dyspnea prompted serial outpatient evaluations, including a pulmonology consultation where resting oxygen saturation was repeatedly measured in the low-to-mid 90s and obesity was documented as a contributing factor. Laboratory testing was ordered as part of the fatigue workup, and iron parameters were discussed with her during follow-up communications. In the context of ongoing symptoms and functional limitation, her primary care provider submitted a request for epoetin alfa injection (HCPCS J0885, 1,000 units, non-ESRD use) with anticipated administration on 06-21-2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital’s Outpatient Infusion Center. The care plan emphasizes monitoring of blood pressure and symptoms around administration given her comorbid hypertension and overall cardiopulmonary status.

276.bio_narrative: Severus Lovegood is a 71-year-old widowed man living independently in Austin, Texas, with close support from his adult daughter who lives nearby and serves as his emergency contact. He previously worked as a high school librarian and remains detail-oriented and health-literate, often bringing carefully kept notes about his symptoms and medications to appointments. Over the past year he has transitioned from an active routine of daily neighborhood walks and community volunteering to a slower pace due to cancer treatment-related fatigue. He carries a diagnosis of metastatic lung cancer (C34.90) with documented liver metastases (C78.7) and has been receiving systemic antineoplastic chemotherapy with regular oncology follow-ups (Z51.11). During chemotherapy cycles, he developed progressively symptomatic anemia characterized by worsening fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and exertional shortness of breath. His recent CBC on 05-08-2026 demonstrated hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL with normocytic indices, consistent with anemia due to antineoplastic chemotherapy (D64.81). At an oncology follow-up on 05-14-2026, he reported needing to rest after climbing one flight of stairs and experiencing intermittent lightheadedness with position changes. He denied overt bleeding symptoms, and prior clinic review of iron and vitamin status did not suggest a primary nutritional cause. Earlier in his treatment course, he received epoetin alfa supportive therapy through the infusion center during a period of similar chemotherapy-associated hemoglobin decline. Infusion center documentation from 12-18-2025 through 01-29-2026 notes he tolerated injections without acute complications and reported improved stamina over time. That prior experience shapes his current preferences to reduce transfusion exposure when possible and maintain his chemotherapy schedule with close monitoring. Socially, he is a former smoker who quit years ago and avoids heavy alcohol. He prioritizes staying functional for daily activities and appointments, though appetite has fluctuated with chemotherapy. Family history is notable for a father with chronic lung disease and a sibling with cancer, which the patient frequently references when discussing his goals of care. Given his active chemotherapy regimen, documented low hemoglobin, persistent symptom burden, and prior response history, his oncology team is requesting epoetin alfa (HCPCS J0885, billed per 1,000 units) with a planned administration date of 06-21-2026, along with serial CBC and blood pressure monitoring.
