Patient has persistent proteinuria and microscopic hematuria with CKD stage 3 while being evaluated for nephritic syndrome. Supportive care with ACE inhibitor therapy and lifestyle measures has been implemented with continued urinary abnormalities. Tarpeyo is requested as targeted therapy with planned objective monitoring of urine protein and renal function.

Objective evidence: 05-02-2026: Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, BUN 28 mg/dL, Potassium 4.6 mmol/L.
pa_request.previous_treatments: ACE inhibitor therapy (lisinopril) with BP optimization; NSAID avoidance; dietary sodium restriction; past fish oil trial stopped due to reflux.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Moderate reduction in proteinuria trend and support stabilization of renal function with monitoring.

286.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 68-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas with her spouse and a small support network of friends. She works part-time in administrative support and tends to push through symptoms until they begin to interfere with her routine. Over the last several months she has become increasingly concerned about foamy urine and persistent fatigue, which prompted a primary care evaluation in late 2025. Initial urinalysis demonstrated both protein and blood in the urine, and she was counseled to avoid NSAIDs and focus on blood pressure control and diet changes while additional testing was arranged. From November 2025 through spring 2026, repeated outpatient assessments continued to show proteinuria (R80.9) and hematuria (R31.9) with renal function trending into chronic kidney disease stage 3 range (N18.3). The working diagnosis remains unspecified nephritic syndrome (N05.9) because a kidney biopsy has not been completed yet. She reports intermittent dull flank discomfort without fever, dysuria, or gross hematuria, and she has had occasional trace ankle swelling after long days on her feet. Her comorbidity of essential hypertension (I10) has been treated with lisinopril and later amlodipine, with home readings that sometimes rise above goal. A renal ultrasound in early May 2026 was obtained to evaluate structural causes of renal decline and to support the diagnostic workup prior to any targeted anti-inflammatory therapy. Imaging demonstrated no hydronephrosis or obstruction and showed mild increased echogenicity consistent with medical renal disease, supporting continued medical management for a glomerular process. She has adhered to NSAID avoidance and has worked with nutrition counseling to lower sodium intake and improve consistency of meals on workdays; a brief fish oil trial was discontinued due to reflux symptoms. Family history is notable for hypertension in both parents and a brother with chronic kidney disease of unclear cause, which increases her concern about progression. Given persistent urinary abnormalities and moderate CKD (N18.3) despite supportive management, nephrology is requesting Tarpeyo (budesonide delayed release) with a structured monitoring plan using repeat renal panels and urine protein measurements. The therapeutic goal is a moderate improvement in proteinuria trend and stabilization of kidney function while maintaining blood pressure control.

Plan: Continue maximized supportive therapy (ACE inhibitor, sodium restriction, BP control) and request Tarpeyo with objective monitoring of urine protein and renal function. Reviewed risks of steroid therapy and need for BP/weight surveillance. Coordinate with PCP and nutrition services.

285.bio_narrative: Anthony Soprano is a 68-year-old male in Houston, Texas, followed by Houston Methodist Primary Care Group and nephrology for newly worked-up hematuria and persistent proteinuria. He has a history of essential hypertension (I10) managed with home monitoring and has been attentive to cardiovascular risk reduction given chronic kidney disease stage 2 (N18.2). He prefers clear, practical recommendations and brings a detailed blood pressure log to visits, asking for objective lab trends to guide next steps. In early 2026, routine urine testing and follow-up evaluation showed persistent microscopic hematuria with proteinuria. He was referred to nephrology and seen on 03-11-2026, where the pattern raised concern for a glomerular process. Supportive measures were reinforced, including sodium restriction and avoidance of nephrotoxins, and his ACE inhibitor therapy was optimized with lisinopril 40 mg daily to address both blood pressure and proteinuria (R80.1). Despite adherence and diet changes, proteinuria persisted, prompting a right native kidney biopsy performed on 04-25-2026. The surgical pathology report dated 04-29-2026 confirmed primary IgA nephropathy (N02.B9), including IgA-dominant mesangial immune deposits and features consistent with ongoing proteinuric disease activity. After biopsy review at follow-up on 05-01-2026, he continued maximized ACE inhibitor therapy and added supportive omega-3 supplementation. He also completed dietitian counseling every two weeks, focusing on sodium reduction and renal-friendly meal planning. Imaging with renal ultrasound on 05-07-2026 showed no obstruction or structural lesion, supporting an intrinsic glomerular etiology. Laboratory monitoring on 05-10-2026 demonstrated stable renal function consistent with CKD stage 2 (N18.2) and ongoing quantified proteinuria above threshold (R80.1) despite optimized supportive therapy. Given biopsy confirmation and persistent proteinuria despite maximized ACE inhibitor therapy and lifestyle measures, nephrology discussed targeted-release budesonide (Tarpeyo) as the next-step therapy. The plan includes continuing lisinopril and ongoing supportive care, while starting Tarpeyo once authorized, with close monitoring of blood pressure, weight/edema, CMP parameters (including glucose and liver enzymes), and serial urine protein quantification to track response. Family history is notable for hypertension in first-degree relatives; he reports elevated stress related to work and family responsibilities but no current depressive or panic symptoms on screening. He is a former smoker, drinks alcohol socially on weekends, denies illicit drug use, and has an engaged home support system through his spouse.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 68-year-old male referred for evaluation of microscopic hematuria and proteinuria noted on recent screening urinalysis. He denies gross hematuria, dysuria, flank pain, fevers, recent URI, or new rash. He reports long-standing hypertension and admits prior frequent NSAID use for aches, now less frequent. Home BP readings run in the 140s/80s. 

Review of Systems: Negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, orthopnea, or significant edema. No weight loss or night sweats. GI: intermittent dyspepsia.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. Abdomen soft, no CVA tenderness. Extremities without significant edema.

Assessment: Persistent hematuria/proteinuria concerning for glomerular disease, including IgA nephropathy (N02.B9 to be confirmed). Hypertension (I10). CKD stage 2 suspected (N18.2) pending labs.

Plan: Order CMP and urine protein quantification to establish baseline renal function and proteinuria severity. Discussed supportive therapy and avoidance of NSAIDs. Increase lisinopril to 40 mg daily (Prinivil) for BP/proteinuria control. Plan for renal ultrasound and proceed to kidney biopsy if proteinuria persists and serologic/structural evaluation supports intrinsic disease.

284.bio_narrative: Ben Kenner is a 72-year-old Hispanic male living in Austin, Texas, followed by Dr. Ahsoka Patel, MD at UT Health Austin for chronic cardiovascular risk management. He has a longstanding history of essential hypertension (I10) with hypertensive heart disease without heart failure (I11.9) and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). He is organized with medications, uses a pill organizer daily, and prefers structured plans and written targets. He is married and has increased caregiving responsibilities that contribute to mild stress; he tends to downplay symptoms unless asked directly. Over the past several months, his home blood pressure readings have remained above goal despite adherence to a three-drug antihypertensive regimen including valsartan, hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine. At the 04-01-2026 office visit, he brought a BP log and described persistent systolic readings in the 140s to 160s, prompting a renewed focus on dietary sodium reduction and consistent measurement technique. In early May 2026, he presented with a chronic dry cough (R05.3), and medication selection was discussed in the context of avoiding cough triggers; he has a known intolerance to ACE inhibitors, with lisinopril previously causing persistent cough and throat irritation. A chest X-ray on 05-03-2026 was obtained to document baseline cardiopulmonary status while optimizing his regimen. During a 05-06-2026 telehealth follow-up, ongoing elevated BP readings were reviewed, and he reported actively reducing sodium, though he still eats restaurant meals frequently. A brief structured lifestyle education session was completed in mid-May to reinforce sodium reduction and a consistent BP logging routine. Baseline laboratory monitoring (BMP and urine microalbumin/creatinine ratio on 05-07-2026) was obtained to support safe medication planning, and a broader baseline panel was reviewed on 05-20-2026 in preparation for the requested finerenone start. Family history is notable for hypertension and premature coronary disease in his father, and hyperlipidemia in a sibling, reinforcing the need for continued risk-factor modification. Ben reports no tobacco or illicit drug use. He describes alcohol as social and occasional, typically on weekends. The current referral/request is for Kerendia (finerenone) 10 mg daily (requested; planned start) as an adjunct to ongoing antihypertensive therapy, with a plan for close monitoring of potassium and renal function after initiation and continued tracking of home BP readings.

pa_request.clinical_justification:
Patient has diabetic CKD with persistent albuminuria and reduced eGFR despite ACE inhibitor therapy. Qualifying objective data (eGFR 38; UACR 620 mg/g) and nephrology treatment history support adding finerenone with potassium/renal monitoring to address ongoing kidney and cardiovascular risk in diabetes and hypertension.

Objective evidence: 05-21-2026: eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73, Potassium 4.8 mmol/L | 05-14-2026: Creatinine 1.6 and, eGFR 38, Potassium 4.8 mmol/L.
supporting_diagnoses: E11.22 - Type 2 diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: ACE inhibitor (lisinopril 20 mg daily, ongoing). Prior SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin 10 mg daily 01-20-2025 to 03-18-2025) discontinued due to recurrent candidiasis and dizziness. Ongoing diabetes optimization with semaglutide and metformin XR; nutrition therapy for low-sodium renal diet.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Reduce albuminuria trajectory and slow CKD progression risk with structured monitoring of potassium and renal function while maintaining ACE inhibitor therapy.

283.bio_narrative: Rosa Diaz is a 69-year-old woman living independently in Austin, Texas. She is direct and pragmatic, keeps a tightly organized routine, and tends to understate symptoms until objective numbers show a change. She worked for many years in a high-stress public-facing job and is now retired, spending time on home projects and caring for a close friend’s family when needed. She is meticulous about taking medications and tracking home blood pressure, but acknowledges that stress can drive her toward packaged, higher-sodium meals. Her current clinical course is centered on chronic kidney disease associated with type 2 diabetes. Over the last 2–3 years, routine labs showed a gradual rise in creatinine and persistent albuminuria. She has remained on maximally tolerated ACE inhibitor therapy (lisinopril) for kidney protection and blood pressure control (I10), and she has worked with her primary care physician to improve glycemic control. Despite these steps, urine testing in early May 2026 again demonstrated significant ongoing protein loss. On 05-02-2026, her renal panel showed creatinine 1.6 mg/dL with eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73 m² and potassium 4.8 mmol/L, and her urine albumin/creatinine ratio was 620 mg/g (R80.9). A1c was 7.6%, consistent with diabetes requiring continued optimization (E11.22). She previously attempted an SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin) as a kidney-protective strategy in 2025; however, she experienced recurrent vulvovaginal yeast infections and symptomatic lightheadedness, leading to discontinuation. She has tolerated semaglutide and a reduced dose of metformin XR in the setting of CKD stage 3b (N18.32). Nephrology evaluated her and confirmed chronic intrinsic disease by renal ultrasound on 05-03-2026, which showed bilateral mildly increased cortical echogenicity and mild cortical thinning without obstruction. Family history is notable for a father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension and a mother with “kidney problems” late in life, which motivates her to be proactive. Given persistent albuminuria and reduced eGFR despite ACE inhibitor therapy, nephrology documented a plan to add finerenone (Kerendia) with structured monitoring of potassium and renal function, alongside continued lifestyle measures and hyperlipidemia management (E78.5).

pa_request.expected_outcome: Moderate improvement in exertional tolerance and congestion stability with ongoing cardiorenal risk reduction and continued monitoring.

282.bio_narrative: April Swanson is a 69-year-old Austin resident who works in a municipal administrative role that keeps her on her feet for meetings and community events. Over the last 8 months, she has noticed a gradual decline in exercise tolerance with shortness of breath when walking more than a block or climbing a single flight of stairs (R06.02). She also reports bilateral ankle swelling that builds over the course of the day and improves partially overnight. She initially attributed symptoms to “being out of shape,” but the persistence and interference with work prompted evaluation. Her primary care visit on 02-28-2026 documented exertional dyspnea and dependent edema in the context of long-standing essential hypertension (I10) and obesity (E66.9). Early management focused on blood pressure control, sodium reduction, and diuretic support. Over the following weeks she had partial relief at rest but continued dyspnea with routine activity. Cardiology consultation on 04-10-2026 and subsequent diagnostic testing showed findings consistent with chronic diastolic (congestive) heart failure (I50.32), including diastolic dysfunction on echocardiogram and clinical congestion on exam. Renal testing identified chronic kidney disease stage 3a (N18.31) with proteinuria (R80.9), emphasizing the need for a cardiorenal treatment strategy and careful medication monitoring. April lives independently and keeps a structured daily routine, often relying on prepared foods due to a busy schedule. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol only occasionally. She has a supportive relationship with her adult son, who is her emergency contact and helps with transportation to appointments. Family history is notable for hypertension in both parents and “heart problems” later in life in her father, which increases her attention to cardiovascular risk reduction. Despite ongoing therapy with losartan, metoprolol succinate ER, and low-dose furosemide plus nutrition counseling, she continues to experience exertional limitations and intermittent edema. Her care team is requesting empagliflozin (Jardiance) as an add-on medication to support management of HFpEF (I50.32) and CKD stage 3a with proteinuria (N18.31, R80.9). The plan includes baseline review of renal function, patient education on hydration and sick-day guidance, and repeat laboratory monitoring within 2–4 weeks after initiation to track electrolytes, kidney function, and tolerability.

pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved glycemic control with reduction in HbA1c over 3 months and additional benefit supporting chronic HFrEF management while continuing guideline-directed therapies.

281.bio_narrative: Severus Diggory is a 72-year-old widowed male living in Dallas, Texas, who presents for coordinated cardiometabolic care. He is a retired high school administrator who continues part-time volunteer work at a community literacy program. Over the last year he has become more deliberate and structured with routines, keeping written logs of blood pressure, daily weights, and glucose readings. He lives alone but has reliable support from his adult niece (listed as next of kin) who assists with transportation to specialty visits. He has a history of Type 2 diabetes mellitus with hyperglycemia (E11.65) and chronic systolic (congestive) heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (I50.22) in the setting of hypertensive heart disease with heart failure (I11.0). In late 2025 he began metformin immediate-release 500 mg twice daily, but despite several months of adherence he continued to note fasting glucose frequently in the 160–190 mg/dL range and experienced persistent gastrointestinal side effects, including loose stools and cramping. He discontinued metformin on 03-18-2026 after discussion with his primary care physician. His laboratory evaluation on 04-29-2026 showed HbA1c 8.1%, fasting glucose 176 mg/dL, creatinine 1.22 mg/dL with eGFR 62 (N18.2), and urine albumin/creatinine ratio consistent with proteinuria (R80.9), supporting the need for therapy adjustment while continuing renal monitoring. From a cardiac standpoint, he reports exertional dyspnea with stairs and occasional ankle swelling that improves with diuretic use. A cardiology consultation and transthoracic echocardiogram on 05-06-2026 documented global hypokinesis with LVEF 32%, establishing HFrEF severity and informing medication optimization. He remains on guideline-directed therapies including sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone, and furosemide, and he participates in Phase II cardiac rehabilitation (CPT 93798) focused on exercise tolerance, symptom monitoring, and sodium restriction. Family history is notable for a father with heart failure and hypertension in later life and a mother with Type 2 diabetes. Given persistent hyperglycemia despite prior metformin trial and the presence of established HFrEF with cardiology support for SGLT2 inhibitor benefit, empagliflozin (Jardiance) is being requested to improve glycemic control and support heart-failure co-management, with planned monitoring for renal function, electrolytes, blood pressure, and volume status.

280.bio_narrative: Ann Perkins is a 69-year-old divorced woman living in Austin, Texas, who has worked for years in a steady, people-facing administrative role and describes herself as detail-oriented, health-focused, and motivated when given a structured plan. Over the last year she has experienced gradual weight gain that has become more noticeable since early 2026, coinciding with worsening right knee pain and a decline in activity. She reports that her knee discomfort makes prolonged walking difficult and she now becomes short of breath when climbing stairs or walking quickly (R06.02). Her main goal is to regain mobility so she can sustain low-impact exercise and participate in daily routines without frequent rest breaks. Her medical history includes morbid obesity due to excess calories (E66.01) with BMI in the 36 range (Z68.36), essential hypertension (I10), hyperlipidemia (E78.5), and unilateral primary osteoarthritis of the right knee (M17.11). She has been adherent with lisinopril and atorvastatin and has used topical diclofenac and acetaminophen to maintain function. She began a calorie-controlled nutrition plan and food logging in spring 2026 and attempted a brief over-the-counter course of orlistat, which she stopped after several days due to bothersome gastrointestinal side effects. She also tried an appetite suppressant in April 2026 but reports minimal benefit and ongoing cravings, particularly in the evening. Her family history is notable for a father with hypertension and hyperlipidemia and a mother with obesity and osteoarthritis; this contributes to her concern about long-term cardiometabolic risk and progressive joint limitation. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially; she denies illicit drug use. She has reliable transportation and an engaged family contact, and she attends appointments consistently when scheduled. In the weeks leading to the current request, she completed clinic visits documenting persistent weight gain and functional limitation and underwent baseline safety and screening tests to support medication initiation. HbA1c was within normal range at 5.4% (R73.09), and CBC/CMP were reviewed as part of a monitoring plan. Imaging documented moderate right knee osteoarthritis and a chest X-ray without acute cardiopulmonary findings, supporting a baseline assessment before initiating weekly semaglutide. Given progressive mobility decline and difficulty achieving weight loss with lifestyle measures alone, she was referred for medication-assisted therapy and a prior authorization request was prepared for Ozempic (semaglutide) injection (CPT J3490) as part of a structured program with ongoing follow-up and monitoring.

encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 69-year-old female with obesity, HTN, HLD presents with several months of weight gain and worsening right knee pain. She reports decreased walking distance and more difficulty with stairs. Denies chest pain; notes mild exertional shortness of breath. She is interested in medical options for weight management.

ROS: Positive for fatigue, weight gain, exertional dyspnea, right knee pain and stiffness. Negative for fever, chest pain, syncope, abdominal pain.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing. Lungs clear. Cardiac RRR. Right knee: medial joint line tenderness and crepitus, no effusion. No LE edema.

Assessment: E66.01 obesity with BMI documented (Z68.36), I10 HTN above goal, E78.5 HLD, M17.11 right knee OA affecting mobility, R06.02 exertional dyspnea.

Plan: Reviewed lifestyle interventions; referred to nutrition counseling and PT for therapeutic exercise (CPT 97110). Ordered right knee X-ray (CPT 73562) for baseline OA assessment. Started phentermine 15 mg daily for short-term appetite suppression; discussed risks and BP monitoring. Continue lisinopril and atorvastatin. Follow up in 4 weeks for weight/BP and to discuss GLP-1 options if needed.

Glipizide ER 10 mg daily since 01-20-2026 with persistent HbA1c 9.2% on 05-06-2026. DSME completed 05-20-2026 with injection training and nutrition plan.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Lower HbA1c and fasting glucose trends with improved cardiometabolic risk factor profile while maintaining safety monitoring and adherence plan.

279.bio_narrative: Connor Roy is a 61-year-old divorced man living in Dallas, Texas, who presents for escalation of treatment for Type 2 diabetes mellitus with hyperglycemia (E11.65). He describes himself as goal-driven and detail-oriented, and he tends to cope with health concerns by seeking highly structured plans. Over the past year he has become increasingly concerned about his long-term health as his home glucose readings remained elevated despite taking his prescribed medications and attempting incremental lifestyle changes. His diabetes management intensified in mid-2025 after repeated fasting readings above goal. He initially started metformin immediate-release but developed persistent gastrointestinal upset and urgency that interfered with daily functioning; this led to a switch to metformin extended-release, which he tolerates better and has taken consistently since 08-01-2025. In January 2026, glipizide ER was added due to ongoing fasting hyperglycemia; he reports using a weekly pill organizer and phone reminders, with only rare missed evening doses when meals run late. Despite this adherence strategy and intermittent efforts to reduce sugary beverages and portion sizes, his laboratory workup on 05-06-2026 showed HbA1c 9.2% and fasting glucose 196 mg/dL. His comorbid obesity (E66.9) is documented with a BMI of 34.0 kg/m², and he carries additional cardiometabolic risk factors including essential hypertension (I10) and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). A urine albumin/creatinine ratio of 48 mg/g is consistent with microalbuminuria/proteinuria (R80.9), increasing concern for early diabetic kidney involvement and reinforcing the need for improved glycemic control and continued ACE-inhibitor therapy. He reports fatigue and polyuria when glucose is high but denies chest pain, dyspnea, or history of pancreatitis. Socially, Connor works in private consulting and travels intermittently, which contributes to reliance on restaurant meals and inconsistent exercise. He drinks alcohol socially and does not use illicit drugs. Family history is notable for a father with Type 2 diabetes and myocardial infarction in his 60s and a mother with hypertension, informing his concern about cardiometabolic complications. After reviewing the objective lab results and treatment course, his endocrinology visit focused on escalation to a GLP-1 receptor agonist, and he completed diabetes self-management education with injection technique training and a home glucose monitoring plan. The current referral requests Ozempic (semaglutide) injection as the next step after dual oral therapy has not achieved adequate glycemic control.
